questions.question: A company currently has an application which is hosted in an On-Demand EC2 instance in one Availability Zone. You are instructed to redesign the architecture to make it scalable and highly available. Which of the following should you do to accomplish this task?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you make an EC2-hosted application scalable and highly available?
choices: Launch an Auto Scaling Group with subnets across 2 AWS regions. Set a minimum, desired, and maximum capacity to 1.
Launch an Auto Scaling Group with subnets across 2 Availability Zones. Set the minimum and maximum capacity to 1.
Launch an Auto Scaling Group with subnets across 3 AWS regions. Set the minimum, desired, and maximum capacity to 2.
Launch an Auto Scaling Group with subnets across 3 Availability Zones. Set the desired and maximum capacity to 5.
correctAnswers: Launch an Auto Scaling Group with subnets across 3 Availability Zones. Set the desired and maximum capacity to 5.
questions.explanation:
Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling enables you to take advantage of the safety and reliability of geographic redundancy by spanning Auto Scaling groups across multiple Availability Zones within a region. When one Availability Zone becomes unhealthy or unavailable, Auto Scaling launches new instances in an unaffected Availability Zone. When the unhealthy Availability Zone returns to a healthy state, Auto Scaling automatically redistributes the application instances evenly across all of the designated Availability Zones.

Hence, the correct answer is: Launch an Auto Scaling Group with subnets across 3 Availability Zones. Set the desired and maximum capacity to 5.

The option that says: Launching an Auto Scaling Group with subnets across 2 Availability Zones and setting the minimum and maximum capacity to 1 is incorrect as setting the maximum capacity to 1 means that the Auto Scaling group will not be able to scale out as required.

The options that say: Launch an Auto Scaling Group with subnets across 2 AWS regions. Set a minimum, desired, and maximum capacity to 1 and Launch an Auto Scaling Group with subnets across 3 AWS regions. Set the minimum, desired, and maximum capacity to 2 are both incorrect because an Auto Scaling group cannot scale out to other AWS regions.

questions.question:
A Junior CloudOps Engineer needs to monitor an ELB for one of the web applications and seeks guidance on where to find the information, such as the client’s IP address, latencies, request paths, and server responses. The engineer also needs to analyze how requests are distributed across targets to verify the routing algorithm is working correctly.

Which of the following options should be used to get the above information?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Where do you find detailed ELB request data like client IPs, latencies, and server responses?
choices: ELB Access Logs
VPC Flow Logs
Amazon CloudFront access logs
Amazon CloudWatch Logs
correctAnswers: ELB Access Logs
questions.explanation:
Elastic Load Balancing provides access logs that capture detailed information about requests sent to your load balancer. Each log contains information such as the time the request was received, the client’s IP address, latencies, request paths, and server responses. You can use these access logs to analyze traffic patterns and troubleshoot issues.

ELB Access Logs

Access logging is an optional feature of Elastic Load Balancing that is disabled by default. After you enable access logging for your load balancer, Elastic Load Balancing captures the logs and stores them in the Amazon S3 bucket that you specify as compressed files. You can disable access logging at any time.

Hence, the correct answer is: ELB Access Logs.

VPC Flow Logs is incorrect because it only captures information about the IP traffic going to and from network interfaces in a VPC. They do not provide the detailed information required to monitor an ELB.

Amazon CloudFront access logs is incorrect because CloudFront access logs record requests made to a CloudFront distribution, not to an Elastic Load Balancer. They include client IPs and request details, but they are specific to CloudFront and cannot be used to monitor ELB traffic.

Amazon CloudWatch Logs is incorrect. Although it can typically be used in conjunction with ELB access logs to monitor and troubleshoot an ELB, it does not provide the detailed information required to monitor an ELB on their own.

questions.question:
A company has two On-Demand EC2 instances in a custom VPC that are launched in two separate subnets named Tango and Delta respectively. The CloudOps Engineer logged into the first instance and was able to reach the HTTP port on the other instance. However, when the Engineer attempted to ping the second instance, it did not respond.

What are the two possible causes of this issue? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Two EC2 instances in different subnets can communicate over HTTP but not via ping. What are two possible causes?
choices: There is no Internet Gateway attached to the VPC.
The second instance's security group does not allow inbound ICMP traffic.
The subnet Tango has no target route to subnet Delta in the route table.
The subnet Delta is private while subnet Tango is public which is why the two instances could not connect to each other.
The NACL on subnet Delta does not allow outbound ICMP traffic.
correctAnswers: The second instance's security group does not allow inbound ICMP traffic.
The NACL on subnet Delta does not allow outbound ICMP traffic.
questions.explanation:
AWS provides two features that you can use to increase security in your VPC: security groups and network ACLs. Security groups control inbound and outbound traffic for your instances, and network ACLs control inbound and outbound traffic for your subnets. In most cases, security groups can meet your needs; however, you can also use network ACLs if you want an additional layer of security for your VPC.

To allow traffic on two EC2 instances located on different subnets, you should properly configure their respective Security Groups as well as the Network ACL.

Hence, the correct answers are:

– The second instance’s security group does not allow inbound ICMP traffic.

– The NACL on subnet Delta does not allow outbound ICMP traffic.

The option that says: The subnet Tango has no target route to subnet Delta in the route table is incorrect because every subnet that you create is automatically associated with the main route table for the VPC. Hence, you don’t need to define a route from subnet Tango to subnet Delta.

The option that says: There is no Internet Gateway attached to the VPC is incorrect because there is no requirement to allow the EC2 instances to connect to the Internet, hence, the use of Internet Gateway (IGW) is unnecessary and totally unrelated to the issue.

The option that says: The subnet Delta is private while subnet Tango is public which is why the two instances could not connect to each other is incorrect because it does not matter whether the subnet is public or private as long as they both reside in one VPC. Take note that a public subnet basically means that it has a route to the Internet Gateway and a private subnet does not. Hence, the subnet type (public/private) is not related to the issue.

questions.question:
A document management system that is hosted in AWS uses an S3 bucket to store its data. Due to a recent cyberattack, the IT Security department mandated that all objects must be encrypted at rest.

Which of the following is a valid option to use to fulfill this requirement? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which two options can encrypt S3 objects at rest?
choices: Use the S3 Bucket Policy to automatically encrypt all objects.
Use the Access Control List (ACL) of the bucket to encrypt all objects in the S3 bucket.
Use AWS server-side encryption for the S3 bucket with Customer-Provided Keys.
Use AWS server-side encryption for the S3 bucket with AWS Managed Keys.
Enable CORS in the S3 bucket.
correctAnswers: Use AWS server-side encryption for the S3 bucket with AWS Managed Keys.
Use AWS server-side encryption for the S3 bucket with Customer-Provided Keys.
questions.explanation:
Server-side encryption is about data encryption at rest—that is, Amazon S3 encrypts your data at the object level as it writes it to disks in its data centers and decrypts it for you when you access it. As long as you authenticate your request and you have access permissions, there is no difference in the way you access encrypted or unencrypted objects. For example, if you share your objects using a presigned URL, that URL works the same way for both encrypted and unencrypted objects.

You have three mutually exclusive options depending on how you choose to manage the encryption keys:

1. Use Server-Side Encryption with Amazon S3-Managed Keys (SSE-S3) – Each object is encrypted with a unique key employing strong multi-factor encryption. As an additional safeguard, it encrypts the key itself with a master key that it regularly rotates. Amazon S3 server-side encryption uses one of the strongest block ciphers available, 256-bit Advanced Encryption Standard (AES-256), to encrypt your data.

2. Use Server-Side Encryption with AWS KMS Keys (SSE-KMS) – Similar to SSE-S3, but with some additional benefits along with some additional charges for using this service. There are separate permissions for the use of an envelope key (that is, a key that protects your data’s encryption key) that provides added protection against unauthorized access to your objects in S3. SSE-KMS also provides you with an audit trail of when your key was used and by whom.

3. Use Server-Side Encryption with Customer-Provided Keys (SSE-C) – You manage the encryption keys, and Amazon S3 manages the encryption, as it writes to disks and decryption when you access your objects.

Hence, the correct answers are:

– Use AWS server-side encryption for the S3 bucket with AWS Managed Keys.

– Use AWS server-side encryption for the S3 bucket with Customer-Provided Keys.

The option that says: Use the S3 Bucket Policy to automatically encrypt all objects is incorrect because the S3 bucket policy cannot be used to automatically encrypt objects. The S3 bucket policy is used to manage permissions and access controls for the bucket and its contents.

The option that says: Use the Access Control List (ACL) of the bucket to encrypt all objects in the S3 bucket is incorrect because the ACL is used to manage permissions for the bucket and its contents. It cannot be used to encrypt objects.

The option that says: Enable CORS in the S3 bucket is incorrect because this is unrelated to encrypting objects in an S3 bucket. CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) is a mechanism that allows many resources on a web page to be requested from another domain outside the domain from which the resource originated.

questions.question:
A startup has a limited budget to spend on AWS services each month. If the CloudOps Engineer overspends it, the following month’s budget will be deducted to cover the additional expenses.

What should the Engineer do to keep the costs in check?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How should a CloudOps Engineer monitor and stay within a fixed monthly AWS spending limit?
choices: Compute expenses regularly using the AWS Pricing Calculator to know how long you should keep your resources running.
Configure AWS Budgets to alert when usage exceeds the allocated budget.
Use Amazon S3 Analytics to analyze and track your AWS bill.
Configure your AWS Budgets to terminate all instances if they start charging beyond your allocated budget.
correctAnswers: Configure AWS Budgets to alert when usage exceeds the allocated budget.
questions.explanation:
AWS Budgets is a tool that allows users to establish custom cost and usage budgets for their AWS resources. This service facilitates the creation of comprehensive budgets based on specific criteria such as monthly costs, service usage, or linked accounts. Users can set thresholds and configure notifications to alert them via email or SMS when their spending or usage approaches or surpasses the budgeted amount. AWS Budgets offers a proactive approach to cost management, assisting organizations in adhering to their financial limits and avoiding unexpected charges.

AWS Budgets

In addition to alerts, AWS Budgets now supports budget control actions, which can automatically enforce cost-saving measures when a budget threshold is breached. For example, users can configure actions to stop or terminate specific AWS resources, adjust Reserved Instance purchases, or restrict certain services to prevent further spending. This feature enhances the ability to manage and control costs effectively, providing a more robust and automated solution for staying within budget. Organizations can leverage AWS Budgets to maintain better financial discipline and optimize their cloud expenditures.

Hence, the correct answer is: Configure AWS Budgets to alert when usage exceeds the allocated budget.

The option that says: Configure your AWS Budgets to terminate all instances if they start charging beyond your allocated budget is something that you should not be considering, especially with a production environment running. Suddenly terminating your instances is detrimental to your system. In addition, the AWS Budgets service is not capable of directly terminating your EC2 instances.

The option that says: Compute your expenses regularly using the AWS Pricing Calculator to know how long you should keep your resources running is useful when planning which AWS resources to use and for how long. However, the computed amount will only be an estimate of what your monthly cost will be.

The option that says: Use Amazon S3 Analytics to analyze and track your AWS bill is incorrect because the S3 Analytics service is just an optional S3 feature used to analyze your object access patterns, and not to analyze your entire AWS bill.

questions.question:
A company launched a health and fitness tracker application that is hosted on a fleet of Spot EC2 Instances in a public subnet of Amazon VPC. As part of security monitoring, the IT Operations team noticed that there are several requests coming from this specific IP address: 137.33.105.110, which originates from a country where they do not have any users. The IT Security department has advised that all subsequent traffic from this IP address should be blocked.

How can the CloudOps Engineer achieve this requirement?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you block all inbound traffic from a specific IP address on your VPC?
choices: Add an Outbound Rule for the Security Group for the Spot EC2 Instances to ensure that no traffic goes to that IP.
Add an Inbound Rule for the NACL which will deny incoming traffic from 137.33.105.110/0.
Add an Inbound Rule for the NACL which will deny incoming traffic from 137.33.105.110/32
Add an Inbound Rule for the Security Group which will deny incoming traffic from 137.33.105.110/24
correctAnswers: Add an Inbound Rule for the NACL which will deny incoming traffic from 137.33.105.110/32
questions.explanation:
A network access control list (NACL) allows or denies specific inbound or outbound traffic at the subnet level. You can use the default network ACL for your VPC, or you can create a custom network ACL for your VPC with rules that are similar to the rules for your security groups in order to add an additional layer of security to your VPC.

Since the request is to block an Inbound request from a specific IP address, it must be stopped at a subnet level. You will need to add a rule to the Network Access Control List. And for a particular IP, you need to use the /32 netmask for the CIDR notation.

Hence, the correct answer is: Add an Inbound Rule for the NACL which will deny incoming traffic from 137.33.105.110/32.

The option that says: Adding an Outbound Rule for the Security Group for the Spot EC2 Instances to ensure that no traffic goes to that IP is incorrect because the rule needs to be added at the subnet level. You can’t explicitly block an IP address in a Security Group though you can allow incoming traffic from a certain IP address.

The option that says: Adding an Inbound Rule for the NACL which will deny incoming traffic from 137.33.105.110/0 is incorrect because 137.33.105.110/0 does not have the correct netmask as you need to declare /32 to only ban the specific IP address and not the entire sub-network.

The option that says: Adding an Inbound Rule for the Security Group which will deny incoming traffic from 137.33.105.110/24 is incorrect because you can’t define an Inbound deny rule for Security Groups. You can only add allow rules to your Security Groups, and after all those allow rules, there is an implicit deny-all rule.

questions.question:
A popular online graphic design tool startup uses a standard S3 bucket that has versioning enabled to store the user-generated images on its platform. They have millions of users around the globe that store their logos, graphics, infographics, and other designs on their platform. Lately, there are a lot of users complaining that they receive a lot of HTTP 503 responses on the platform.

Which of the following options could be the reason why this issue exists?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Why would an S3 bucket with versioning enabled return frequent HTTP 503 errors?
choices: You might have one or more objects in the bucket for which there are millions of versions.
S3 could not handle simultaneous access to the bucket since the S3 transfer acceleration option is not enabled.
The Cross-Region Replication (CRR) option is not enabled, which is required if the S3 bucket is being accessed from multiple regions.
The HTTP 404 NoSuchKey error is being returned frequently, which could be mistaken for 503 errors by the application.
correctAnswers: You might have one or more objects in the bucket for which there are millions of versions.
questions.explanation:
If you notice a significant increase in the number of HTTP 503-slowdown responses received for Amazon S3 PUT or DELETE object requests to a bucket that has versioning enabled, you might have one or more objects in the bucket for which there are millions of versions. When you have objects with millions of versions, Amazon S3 automatically throttles requests to the bucket to protect the customer from an excessive amount of request traffic, which could potentially impede other requests made to the same bucket.

Creating inventory config

To determine which S3 objects have millions of versions, use the Amazon S3 inventory tool. The inventory tool generates a report that provides a flat file list of the objects in a bucket.

Hence, the correct answer is: You might have one or more objects in the bucket for which there are millions of versions.

The option that says: The HTTP 404 NoSuchKey error is being returned frequently, which could be mistaken for 503 errors by the application is incorrect because an HTTP 404 NoSuchKey error indicates the object does not exist (client error 4xx), not a server error. Repeated 404s do not cause 503 responses.

The option that says: S3 could not handle simultaneous access to the bucket since the S3 transfer acceleration option is not enabled is incorrect because S3 is primarily designed to handle simultaneous access to the bucket, and enabling S3 Transfer Acceleration would not solve this issue.

The option that says: The Cross-Region Replication (CRR) option is not enabled, which is required if the S3 bucket is being accessed from multiple regions is incorrect because Cross-Region Replication is not required in this scenario and would not just resolve the HTTP 503 response issue.

questions.question:
A former colleague reached out to you for consultation. He uploads a Django project in Elastic Beanstalk through CLI using instructions he read in a blog post, but for some reason he could not create the environment he needs for his project. He encounters an error message saying “The instance profile aws-elasticbeanstalk-ec2-role associated with the environment does not exist.”

What are the possible causes of this issue? (SELECT TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What are two reasons the Elastic Beanstalk EC2 instance profile does not exist error occurs?
choices: Elastic Beanstalk CLI did not create one because your IAM role has no permission to create roles.
You have not associated an Elastic Beanstalk role to your CLI.
Instance profile container for the role needs to be manually replaced every time a new environment is launched.
IAM role already exists but has insufficient permissions that Elastic Beanstalk needs.
He selected the wrong platform for the Django code.
correctAnswers: Elastic Beanstalk CLI did not create one because your IAM role has no permission to create roles.
IAM role already exists but has insufficient permissions that Elastic Beanstalk needs.
questions.explanation:
AWS EB CLI cannot create the instance profile for your Beanstalk environment if your IAM role has no access to creating roles.

This error is also thrown when the instance profile has insufficient or outdated policies that Beanstalk needs to function. More details on this can be seen in the references provided.

The error message indicates that the instance profile associated with the environment does not exist, which means that the role used to launch the environment does not have permission to create roles.

Even if the role used to launch the environment has permission to create roles, the instance profile may have insufficient permissions that Elastic Beanstalk needs to launch the environment.

Hence, the correct answers are:

– Elastic Beanstalk CLI did not create one because your IAM role has no permission to create roles.

– IAM role already exists but has insufficient permissions that Elastic Beanstalk needs.

The option that says: He selected the wrong platform for the Django code is incorrect because this would give a different issue.

The option that says: Instance profile container for the role needs to be manually replaced every time a new environment is launched is incorrect because there is no need to manually replace instance profile containers.

The option that says: You have not associated an Elastic Beanstalk role to your CLI is incorrect because logging in to the CLI also assumes the role in your account.

questions.question:
A shoe company provisioned an Amazon Aurora database cluster with MySQL support. In addition, backtracking, point-in-time recovery, and automatic backup are enabled for the database cluster. However, a developer mistakenly performed a DELETE query without a WHERE clause on the database. The CloudOps Engineer needs to roll back the database cluster to a specific point in time within the last 72 hours and ensure that the database restore procedure will be done on the same DB instance and not by creating a new one.

What should the CloudOps Engineer do to restore the database?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you roll back an Aurora cluster to a specific point in time without creating a new DB instance?
choices: Create a backup plan in AWS Backup that runs every 72 hours.
Rewind the Aurora database cluster to the desired recovery point by using backtracking.
Utilize point-in-time recovery to restore the database instance to a specified point in time
Build an Aurora Replica and promote it to be the primary database instance.
correctAnswers: Rewind the Aurora database cluster to the desired recovery point by using backtracking.
questions.explanation:
Backtracking “rewinds” the DB cluster to the time you specify. Backtracking is not a replacement for backing up your DB cluster so that you can restore it to a point in time. However, backtracking provides the following advantages over traditional backup and restore:

– You can easily undo mistakes. If you mistakenly perform a destructive action, such as a DELETE without a WHERE clause, you can backtrack the DB cluster to a time before the destructive action with minimal interruption of service.

– You can backtrack a DB cluster quickly. Restoring a DB cluster to a point in time launches a new DB cluster and restores it from backup data or a DB cluster snapshot, which can take hours. Backtracking a DB cluster doesn’t require a new DB cluster and rewinds the DB cluster in minutes.

Hence, the correct answer is: Rewind the Aurora database cluster to the desired recovery point by using backtracking.

The option that says: Build an Aurora Replica and promote it to be the primary database instance is incorrect because Aurora Replica is mainly used when you want to offload read workloads from an Aurora database.

The option that says: Create a backup plan in AWS Backup that runs every 72 hours is incorrect because restoring a database cluster through AWS Backup would mean that new database instances will be created. The requirement is to ensure that you are performing the restore procedure on the same database.

The option that says: Utilize point-in-time recovery to restore the database instance to a specified point in time is incorrect because doing a point-in-time recovery in Amazon Aurora means restoring the database to a new DB cluster from a snapshot. Take note that the requirement is to ensure the restore procedure is done on the same DB instance.

questions.question:
An application is deployed in AWS by creating a CloudFormation stack. A junior engineer accidentally updated production resources during a stack change set execution, causing significant downtime. This issue prompted the DevOps team to modify the deployment process. The CloudOps Engineer still wants to allow the team to deploy changes to the infrastructure. Still, he needs to ensure that he can prevent accidental changes on specific production resources.

What should the CloudOps Engineer perform to achieve the requirement?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you prevent accidental updates to specific CloudFormation stack resources in production?
choices: Define a stack policy that explicitly allows updates on all resources but denies Update:* actions on protected resources. Set the newly created stack policy on the existing CloudFormation stack for the production environment.

Create an Amazon CloudWatch event that triggers based on CloudFormation API calls. Use AWS Lambda to describe the stack and determine protected resources that are modified, then negate the actions performed on them if necessary.

Create a custom AWS Config rule that alerts based on changes to any Cloud Formation stack. Use AWS Lambda to describe the stack and determine protected resources that are modified, then negate the actions performed on them if necessary.

Define an IAM policy that restricts a CloudFormation stack from performing update actions with a condition on the specific Amazon Resource Names (ARNs) of the protected resources. Attach the policy to the DevOps team.
correctAnswers: Define a stack policy that explicitly allows updates on all resources but denies Update:* actions on protected resources. Set the newly created stack policy on the existing CloudFormation stack for the production environment.
questions.explanation:
During a stack update, AWS CloudFormation automatically updates resources that depend on other updated resources. For example, AWS CloudFormation updates a resource that references an updated resource. AWS CloudFormation makes no physical changes, such as the resource ID, to automatically update resources, but if a stack policy is associated with those resources, you must have permission to update them.

When you create a stack, no stack policy is set, so all update actions are allowed on all resources. To protect stack resources from update actions, define a stack policy and then set it on your stack. A stack policy is a JSON document that defines the AWS CloudFormation stack update actions that AWS CloudFormation users can perform and the resources that the actions apply to. You set the stack policy when you create a stack, by specifying a text file that contains your stack policy or typing it out. When you set a stack policy on your stack, any update not explicitly allowed is denied by default.

Hence, the correct answer is: Define a stack policy that explicitly allows updates on all resources but denies Update:* actions on protected resources. Set the newly created stack policy on the existing CloudFormation stack for the production environment.

The option that says: Create a custom AWS Config rule that alerts based on changes to any Cloud Formation stack. Use AWS Lambda to describe the stack and determine protected resources that are modified, then negate the actions performed on them if necessary is incorrect because an AWS Config rule only helps you detect configurations on your AWS resources that are non-compliant with your desired resource configuration.

The option that says: Create an Amazon CloudWatch event that triggers based on CloudFormation API calls. Use AWS Lambda to describe the stack and determine protected resources that are modified, then negate the actions performed on them if necessary is incorrect because you’ll have to set up an event for each resource in the organization which means that there will be too many API calls to monitor. Instead, you can use a stack policy to restrict users from performing updates on selected resources.

The option that says: Define an IAM policy that restricts a CloudFormation stack from performing update actions with a condition on the specific Amazon Resource Names (ARNs) of the protected resources. Attach the policy to the DevOps team is incorrect. The IAM policy described in the option can only prevent users from updating the resources, but it will still allow users with a higher level of permission to update the resources. You need to be able to protect the resources from getting updated by any account which makes this option incorrect.

questions.question:
A company is faced with the challenge of connecting two crucial applications. One application resides in an on-premises data center, identified with hostname tdojo.local private. The second application operates on an Amazon EC2 instance with the hostname tdojo.aws-cloud private. The company has an AWS Direct Connect connection linking the on-premises network to AWS. Despite this, the on-premises application is still unable to reach the EC2 instance due to DNS resolution failures.

The CloudOps Engineer must implement a solution to enable seamless DNS resolution between resources in the on-premises data center and AWS.

Which solution would enable the on-premises application to successfully resolve the hostname of the EC2 instance?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you enable on-premises DNS to resolve AWS private hostnames over Direct Connect?
choices: Configure an Amazon Route 53 Resolver outbound endpoint. Choose the VPC where the EC2 instance is hosted. Configure the on-premises DNS resolver to direct local private DNS queries towards the newly established outbound resolver endpoint.

Configure an Amazon Route 53 Resolver inbound endpoint. Choose the VPC where the EC2 instance is hosted. Configure the on-premises DNS resolver to direct aws-cloud private DNS queries toward the newly established inbound resolver endpoint.

Configure an Amazon Route 53 Resolver outbound endpoint. Choose the VPC where the EC2 instance is hosted. Configure the on-premises DNS resolver to direct aws-cloud private DNS queries toward the newly established outbound resolver endpoint.

Configure an Amazon Route 53 Resolver inbound endpoint. Choose the VPC where the EC2 instance is hosted. Configure the on-premises DNS resolver to direct local private DNS queries toward the newly established inbound resolver endpoint.
correctAnswers: Configure an Amazon Route 53 Resolver inbound endpoint. Choose the VPC where the EC2 instance is hosted. Configure the on-premises DNS resolver to direct aws-cloud private DNS queries toward the newly established inbound resolver endpoint.
questions.explanation:
Route 53 Resolver is a regional DNS service that provides recursive DNS lookups for names hosted in EC2 as well as public names on the internet. This functionality is available by default in every Amazon Virtual Private Cloud (VPC).

If you have workloads that leverage both VPCs and on-premises resources, you need to resolve DNS records hosted on-premises. Similarly, these on-premises resources may need to resolve names hosted on AWS. Through Resolver endpoints and conditional forwarding rules, you can resolve DNS queries between your on-premises resources and VPCs to create a hybrid cloud setup over VPN or Direct Connect (DX).

Before you start to forward queries, you must create a Resolver inbound and/or outbound endpoints in the connected VPC. These endpoints provide a path for inbound or outbound queries:

Inbound endpoint: DNS resolvers on your network can forward DNS queries to Route 53 Resolver via this endpoint. This allows your DNS resolvers to easily resolve domain names for AWS resources such as EC2 instances or records in a Route 53 private hosted zone.

The following diagram shows the path of a DNS query from a DNS resolver on your network to Route 53 Resolver endpoints.

Outbound endpoint: Resolver conditionally forwards queries to resolvers on your network via this endpoint. To forward selected queries, you create Resolver rules that specify the domain names for the DNS queries that you want to forward (such as example.com) and the IP addresses of the DNS resolvers on your network that you want to forward the queries to.

The following diagram shows the path of a DNS query from an EC2 instance in one of your VPCs to a DNS resolver on your network.

From the scenario, the on-premises application is encountering difficulties connecting to the EC2 instance due to DNS resolution failures. To resolve the failure, you must set up an Amazon Route 53 resolver inbound endpoint to successfully resolve the DNS queries for the EC2 instance. Additionally, you must configure the on-premises DNS resolver to forward aws-cloud private DNS queries to the Amazon Route 53 resolver inbound endpoint.

Hence, the correct answer is: Configure an Amazon Route 53 Resolver inbound endpoint. Choose the VPC where the EC2 instance is hosted. Configure the on-premises DNS resolver to direct aws-cloud private DNS queries toward the newly established inbound resolver endpoint

The option that says: Configure an Amazon Route 53 Resolver inbound endpoint. Choose the VPC where the EC2 instance is hosted. Configure the on-premises DNS resolver to direct local private DNS queries toward the newly established inbound resolver endpoint is incorrect. While configuring an Amazon Route 53 Resolver inbound endpoint is a step in the right direction, this solution will still fail the DNS resolution because the on-premises DNS resolver is forwarding the on-prem private DNS queries instead of aws-cloud private. There must be a forwarding rule for aws-cloud private in the on-premises DNS resolver to solve the issue.

The option that says: Configure an Amazon Route 53 Resolver outbound endpoint. Choose the VPC where the EC2 instance is hosted. Configure the on-premises DNS resolver to direct local private DNS queries towards the newly established outbound resolver endpoint is incorrect because the direction of the DNS queries in the scenario is going to the AWS cloud. Therefore, the AWS Route 53 resolver endpoint type that you need to configure is an inbound endpoint, not an outbound endpoint.

The option that says: Configure an Amazon Route 53 Resolver outbound endpoint. Choose the VPC where the EC2 instance is hosted. Configure the on-premises DNS resolver to direct aws-cloud private DNS queries toward the newly established outbound resolver endpoint is incorrect. Just like the previous option, this solution will not resolve the issue because the configured endpoint is an outbound instead of an inbound endpoint. Even though from the on-premises side, it is correct to forward the aws-cloud private DNS queries. However, it must point to an inbound endpoint, not an outbound endpoint.

questions.simplifiedQuestion: What happens when an S3 bucket policy grants s3:GetObject and s3:ListBucket on a bucket ARN without the /* suffix?
choices: You will be prompted with an "Action does not apply to any resource(s) in statement" error.
The tutorialsdojo bucket including all its objects will be publicly accessible and downloadable to anyone.
The tutorialsdojo bucket including all its objects will be publicly visible to anyone.
The tutorialsdojo bucket including all its objects will be publicly visible to anyone but downloading the objects is not allowed.
correctAnswers: You will be prompted with an "Action does not apply to any resource(s) in statement" error.
questions.explanation:
In this scenario, you will be prompted with an “Action does not apply to any resource(s) in statement” error if you tried to apply this bucket policy. The statement is using the s3:GetObject action, which means that you should add the specific object to the bucket. If this is not declared then an error will be thrown.

To fix the policy, you simply have to specify two items under the Resource section: one for the s3:GetObject action and another one for the s3:ListBucket action. Take note that the s3:GetObject action is using a wildcard ( /* ) which refers to all of the objects in the bucket. The mapping is as follows:

s3:GetObject = arn:aws:s3:::tutorialsdojo/*

s3:ListBucket = arn:aws:s3:::tutorialsdojo

Hence, the correct answer is: You will be prompted with an “Action does not apply to any resource(s) in statement” error.

The option that says: The tutorialsdojo bucket including all its objects will be publicly visible to anyone but downloading the objects is not allowed and The tutorialsdojo bucket including all its objects will be publicly visible to anyone are both incorrect because the bucket and/or objects in the bucket will be publicly visible and/or accessible. However, this is not the case with the policy provided.

The option that says: The tutorialsdojo bucket including all its objects will be publicly accessible and downloadable to anyone is incorrect because this is not the case either, as the policy is configured to allow only the s3:GetObject and s3:ListBucket actions for all principals (“*”) on the tutorialsdojo bucket and the Resource field specifies the tutorialsdojo bucket but not any specific object or subfolder within the bucket.

questions.question:
A CloudOps Engineer needs to secure an Amazon RDS MySQL database instance which is being used by an Auto Scaling group of On-Demand EC2 instances in a VPC. The Engineer also has to make sure that the database traffic is encrypted to meet the security compliance requirements.

Which of the following should you do to ensure maximum security for the database and its connections? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which two measures secure an RDS MySQL instance and encrypt its traffic within a VPC?
choices: Create a snapshot of the running MySQL instance and start a new encrypted MySQL instance from the encrypted copy of the snapshot.

Create a security group for the servers and another security group for the RDS MySQL database. Configure to only allow inbound traffic from the security group of the EC2 instances to the security group of the database.
Enable VPC Flow Logs.
Stop your MySQL instance and select instance actions. From there, click Enable Encryption to allow encryption on the data stored in the database.
Create a dummy database to confuse attackers.
correctAnswers: Create a snapshot of the running MySQL instance and start a new encrypted MySQL instance from the encrypted copy of the snapshot.

Create a security group for the servers and another security group for the RDS MySQL database. Configure to only allow inbound traffic from the security group of the EC2 instances to the security group of the database.
questions.explanation:
Amazon RDS conforms to the AWS shared responsibility model, which includes regulations and guidelines for data protection. AWS is responsible for protecting the global infrastructure that runs all the AWS services. AWS maintains control over data hosted on this infrastructure, including the security configuration controls for handling customer content and personal data. AWS customers and APN partners, acting either as data controllers or data processors, are responsible for any personal data that they put in the AWS Cloud.

You can use Secure Socket Layer (SSL) or Transport Layer Security (TLS) from your application to encrypt a connection to a DB instance running MySQL, MariaDB, SQL Server, Oracle, or PostgreSQL. Each DB engine has its own process for implementing SSL/TLS.

Security groups control the access that traffic has in and out of a DB instance. Three types of security groups are used with Amazon RDS: DB security groups, VPC security groups, and Amazon EC2 security groups. In simple terms, these work as follows:

– A DB security group controls access to EC2-Classic DB instances that are not in a VPC.

– A VPC security group controls access to DB instances and EC2 instances inside a VPC.

– An EC2 security group controls access to an EC2 instance.

By default, network access is disabled for a DB instance. You can specify rules in a security group that allows access from an IP address range, port, or EC2 security group. Once ingress rules are configured, the same rules apply to all DB instances that are associated with that security group. You can specify up to 20 rules in a security group.

You can only enable encryption for an Amazon RDS DB instance when you create it, not after the DB instance is created. However, because you can encrypt a copy of an unencrypted DB snapshot, you can effectively add encryption to an unencrypted DB instance. That is, you can create a snapshot of your DB instance and then create an encrypted copy of that snapshot. You can then restore a DB instance from the encrypted snapshot, and thus you have an encrypted copy of your original DB instance.

Hence, the correct answers are:

– Create a snapshot of the running MySQL instance and start a new encrypted MySQL instance from the encrypted copy of the snapshot.

– Create a security group for the servers and another security group for the RDS MySQL database. Configure to only allow inbound traffic from the security group of the EC2 instances to the security group of the database.

The option that says: Stop your MySQL instance and select instance actions. From there, click Enable Encryption to allow encryption on the data stored in the database is incorrect because you cannot encrypt a database after creation, however, you can take a snapshot of the created database and encrypt a copy of the snapshot instead.

Creating a dummy database to confuse attackers is incorrect because creating another database as a dummy won’t protect your data. You’ll also be charged more if you do this.

Enabling VPC Flow Logs is incorrect because this is just a feature that enables you to capture information about the IP traffic going to and from network interfaces in your VPC. You won’t be able to fully protect your database using this feature. Flow log data can be published to Amazon CloudWatch Logs or Amazon S3. After you’ve created a flow log, you can retrieve and view its data in the chosen destination.

questions.question:
A money transfer mobile app is heavily using RESTful web services, which are hosted in an Auto Scaling group of Spot Amazon EC2 instances across multiple Availability Zones and a Load Balancer. The monitoring system is being set up, and it is known that the web services currently utilize a significant amount of memory in order to function properly.

Which of the following should be implemented to properly monitor the memory usage of the EC2 instances?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you monitor memory utilization on EC2 instances using Amazon CloudWatch?
choices: Enable Basic Monitoring to view the Memory Utilization of each EC2 instance.
Enable Detailed Monitoring to view the Memory Utilization of each EC2 instance.

Set up and install the Amazon CloudWatch agent on all EC2 instances. Set up a custom metric to view the various memory utilization metrics of each EC2 instance, such as mem_available, mem_cached, mem_active, and many others.

Install the AWS X-Ray daemon on all EC2 instances. Set up a custom metric to view the various memory utilization metrics of each EC2 instance, such as mem_available, mem_cached, mem_active, and many others.
correctAnswers: Set up and install the Amazon CloudWatch agent on all EC2 instances. Set up a custom metric to view the various memory utilization metrics of each EC2 instance, such as mem_available, mem_cached, mem_active, and many others.
questions.explanation:
Amazon CloudWatch is a web service that enables you to monitor, manage, and publish various metrics, as well as configure alarm actions based on data from metrics. You can define custom metrics for your own use, and Elastic Beanstalk will push those metrics to Amazon CloudWatch. Once Amazon CloudWatch contains your custom metrics, you can view them in the Amazon CloudWatch console.

The unified CloudWatch agent enables you to do the following:

– Collect more system-level metrics from Amazon EC2 instances across operating systems. The metrics can include in-guest metrics in addition to the metrics for EC2 instances.

– Collect system-level metrics from on-premises servers. These can include servers in a hybrid environment, as well as servers not managed by AWS.

– Retrieve custom metrics from your applications or services using the StatsD and collectd protocols. StatsD is supported on both Linux servers and servers running Windows Server. collectd is supported only on Linux servers.

– Collect logs from Amazon EC2 instances and on-premises servers running either Linux or Windows Server.

CloudWatch Agent

Take note that there is no built-in “Memory Utilization” metric available in CloudWatch for EC2. You have to set up a custom metric to track the memory usage of your EC2 instances.

Hence, the correct answer is: Set up and install the Amazon CloudWatch agent on all EC2 instances. Set up a custom metric to view the various memory utilization metrics of each EC2 instance, such as mem_available, mem_cached, mem_active, and many others

The option that says: Enable Detailed Monitoring to view the Memory Utilization of each EC2 instance is incorrect because detailed monitoring will only increase the frequency of data points in the metrics. If this feature is enabled, the Amazon EC2 console will display the monitoring graph with a 1-minute period for the instance.

The option that says: Enable Basic Monitoring to view the Memory Utilization of each EC2 instance is incorrect because Basic Monitoring is already enabled by default. Furthermore, there is no built-in Memory Utilization in CloudWatch. You have to install the CloudWatch agent in your instances and create a custom metric to view the memory usage of your instances.

The option that says: Install the AWS X-Ray daemon on all EC2 instances. Set up a custom metric to view the various memory utilization metrics of each EC2 instance, such as mem_available, mem_cached, mem_active, and many others. is incorrect because an X-Ray daemon is just a software application that listens for traffic on UDP port 2000, gathers raw segment data, and relays it to the AWS X-Ray for debugging. You have to install the CloudWatch agent instead.

questions.question:
A tech lead plans to deploy a highly available web application on 12 Amazon EC2 instances. She is required to deploy each of the instances on distinct underlying hardware.

What solution should the tech lead implement to meet the requirements?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which EC2 placement group strategy places every instance on distinct underlying hardware?
choices: Deploy the instances in multiple regions and place them into a partition placement group.
Use the cluster placement group when launching the EC2 instances and place them in a single region.
Use the spread placement group when launching the EC2 instances and deploy them in multiple regions.
Deploy the instances in a single region and place them into a spread placement group.
correctAnswers: Deploy the instances in a single region and place them into a spread placement group.
questions.explanation:
You can use placement groups to influence the placement of a group of interdependent instances to meet the needs of your workload. Depending on the type of workload, you can create a placement group using one of the following placement strategies:

Cluster – packs instances close together inside an Availability Zone. This strategy enables workloads to achieve the low-latency network performance necessary for tightly-coupled node-to-node communication that is typical of HPC applications.

Partition – spreads your instances across logical partitions such that groups of instances in one partition do not share the underlying hardware with groups of instances in different partitions. This strategy is typically used by large distributed and replicated workloads, such as Hadoop, Cassandra, and Kafka.

Spread – strictly places a small group of instances across distinct underlying hardware to reduce correlated failures.

A spread placement group is a group of instances that are each placed on distinct racks, with each rack having its own network and power source.

The following image shows seven instances in a single Availability Zone that are placed into a spread placement group. The seven instances are placed on seven different racks.

Spread placement groups are recommended for applications that have a small number of critical instances that should be kept separate from each other. Launching instances in a spread placement group reduces the risk of simultaneous failures that might occur when instances share the same racks. Spread placement groups provide access to distinct racks, and are therefore suitable for mixing instance types or launching instances over time.

A spread placement group can span multiple Availability Zones in the same Region. You can have a maximum of seven running instances per Availability Zone per group.

If you start or launch an instance in a spread placement group and there is insufficient unique hardware to fulfill the request, the request fails. Amazon EC2 makes more distinct hardware available over time, so you can try your request again later.

Hence, the correct answer is: Deploy the instances in a single region and place them into a spread placement group.

The option that says: Use the cluster placement group when launching the EC2 instances and place them in a single region is incorrect because it just packs instances close together inside an Availability Zone. The tech lead is required to create a highly available infrastructure.

The option that says: Deploy the instances in multiple regions and place them into a partition placement group is incorrect because this just spreads your instances across logical partitions such that groups of instances in one partition do not share the underlying hardware with groups of instances in different partitions. You need each instance to have distinct underlying hardware which makes this option incorrect.

The option that says: Use the spread placement group when launching the EC2 instances and deploy them in multiple regions is incorrect because a spread placement group is just limited to a single AWS region. You can’t deploy a spread placement group to multiple AWS regions.

questions.question:
An electronics manufacturing company has recently decided to adopt a hybrid cloud infrastructure that will store its backup data from its on-premises data center to AWS. The company has uploaded archive files, totaling 70 TB, to Amazon Glacier using the AWS CLI. A file named tutorialsdojo.zip was uploaded to Glacier, resulting in the following response shown below. However, it is observed that a custom key name, such as tutorialsdojo.zip, cannot be assigned to the archives uploaded to Glacier. { "archiveId": "kKB7ymWJVpPSwhGP6ycSOAekp9ZYe_--zM_mw6k76ZFGEIWQX-ybtRDvc2VkPSDtfKmQrj0IRQLSGsNuDp-AJVlu2ccmDSyDUmZwKbwbpAdGATGDiB3hHO0bjbGehXTcApVud_wyDw", "checksum": "969fb39823836d81f0cc028195fcdbcbbe76cdde932d4646fa7de5f21e18aa67", "location": "/0123456789012/vaults/my-vault/archives/kKB7ymWJVpPSwhGP6ycSOAekp9ZYe_--zM_mw6k76ZFGEIWQX-ybtRDvc2VkPSDtfKmQrj0IRQLSGsNuDp-AJVlu2ccmDSyDUmZwKbwbpAdGATGDiB3hHO0bjbGehXTcApVud_wyDw" }

Which of the following options can ensure that the same file is stored in Glacier in the most cost-effective way? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What are two cost-effective ways to store files in Glacier while preserving their original filenames?
choices: Set up an Amazon DynamoDB table and create a mapping of all the archive filenames to each archive ID in Glacier.
Use Amazon ElastiCache for Memcached with Geospatial support to transfer the archives to Glacier.
Upload the archive files to Amazon S3 Infrequent Access. Set up a lifecycle policy to move the archives to Glacier.
Use AWS DataSync to transfer the archive files to Glacier by using the Amazon S3 lifecycle policy.
Use the AWS Glacier Console to upload the archive file directly to Amazon S3 Glacier.
correctAnswers: Use AWS DataSync to transfer the archive files to Glacier by using the Amazon S3 lifecycle policy.
Upload the archive files to Amazon S3 Infrequent Access. Set up a lifecycle policy to move the archives to Glacier.
questions.explanation:
In Amazon S3 Glacier, an archive is any object, such as a photo, video, or document that you store in a vault. It is a base unit of storage in Glacier. Each archive has a unique ID and an optional description. When you upload an archive, Amazon S3 Glacier returns a response that includes an archive ID. This archive ID is unique in the region in which the archive is stored.

In Amazon S3 Glacier, you cannot assign a key name to the archives that you upload. Except for the optional archive description, Glacier does not support any additional metadata for the archives. When you upload an archive, Amazon S3 Glacier assigns an ID, which is an opaque sequence of characters, from which you cannot infer any meaning about the archive. You might maintain metadata about the archives on the client-side.

Amazon S3 - Lifecycle configuration

DataSync can efficiently move large datasets to Amazon S3, preserving filenames. Lifecycle policies can then automatically transition the files to Glacier, ensuring cost-effectiveness and filename retention. Storing files in S3 first preserves their filenames. Lifecycle policies can then automatically move them to Glacier cost-effectively, eliminating the need to manually track archive IDs.

Hence, the correct answers are:

– Use AWS DataSync to transfer the archive files to Glacier by using the Amazon S3 lifecycle policy.

– Upload the archive files to Amazon S3 Infrequent Access. Set up a lifecycle policy to move the archives to Glacier.

The option that says: Set up an Amazon DynamoDB table and create a mapping of all the archive filenames to each archive ID in Glacier is incorrect. Setting up a separate DynamoDB table to map the archive IDs is not cost-effective, as you have to pay the running cost of maintaining your DynamoDB instance. You can just use Amazon S3 instead.

The option that says: Use the AWS Glacier Console to upload the archive file directly to Amazon S3 Glacier is incorrect because the result will still be the same even if you use the Amazon S3 Glacier Console.

The option that says: Use Amazon ElastiCache for Memcached with Geospatial support to transfer the archives to Glacier is incorrect because Amazon ElastiCache is typically used for caching purposes and not for data transfer. Moreover, Memcached doesn’t have Geospatial support. This is only available in Amazon ElastiCache for Redis.

questions.question:
A large accounting firm has a web application that consists of an Application Load Balancer and an Auto Scaling Group of EC2 Instances running Linux and Apache HTTP servers. For its database tier, they also have a running RDS instance to host the MySQL database.

Which security measures fall within AWS responsibilities?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which security task is AWS's responsibility under the shared responsibility model for EC2 and RDS?
choices: Protect against network packet sniffing.
Protect the EC2 instances against attacks by enforcing the principle of least privilege access.
Install the latest security patches on EC2 instances.
Ensure all communication between EC2 and ELB is encrypted.
correctAnswers: Protect against network packet sniffing.
questions.explanation:
Protecting against network packet sniffing is the correct answer. Please see the below AWS shared responsibility model. This falls on the Networking responsibility of AWS. Packet sniffing usually relies on network interfaces that are in promiscuous mode to sniff all traffic even those not intended for the instance. AWS hypervisor will not deliver any traffic to an instance that is not addressed to it.

Protecting the EC2 instances against attacks by enforcing the principle of least privilege access is incorrect because as per the above diagram, protecting the EC2 instances via security groups is the responsibility of the customer.

Ensuring all communication between EC2 and ELB is encrypted is incorrect because encrypting the traffic between EC2 and ELB is optional.

Installing the latest security patches on EC2 instances is incorrect because it is the responsibility of the customer.

questions.question:
A CloudOps Engineer recently finished setting up a new virtual private cloud with a size of /16 IPv4 CIDR block, including one subnet with a size /24 IPv4 CIDR block in AWS. The Engineer also launched an On-Demand Amazon EC2 instance with an NGINX AMI from the AWS Marketplace, which will be used to host the company’s WordPress website. The security group of the instance has been modified to enable inbound traffic from port 22 to allow the Administrator to connect to it using SSH. The Administrator attempted to connect to the instance, but it failed to establish a connection. The Administrator added an Internet Gateway (igw-1a2b3c4d) in the VPC, yet the problem still persists. The main route table has two entries as shown below:

DESTINATION TARGET 10.0.0.0/16 local 10.0.0.0/16 igw-1a2b3c4d Which of the following options can the Administrator do to solve this issue?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: An Internet Gateway is attached to a VPC but SSH to an EC2 instance still fails — what route table fix is needed?
choices: Attach an Elastic IP to the Instance
Change the destination of the local target to 0.0.0.0/0
Attach a NAT gateway to the VPC
Change the destination of the IGW to 0.0.0.0/0
correctAnswers: Change the destination of the IGW to 0.0.0.0/0
questions.explanation:
An internet gateway is a horizontally scaled, redundant, and highly available VPC component that allows communication between your VPC and the internet. It supports IPv4 and IPv6 traffic. It does not cause availability risks or bandwidth constraints on your network traffic.

Internet gateway

The first entry is the default entry for local IPv4 routing in the VPC; this entry enables the instances in this VPC to communicate with each other. The second entry routes all other IPv4 subnet traffic to the Internet gateway (igw-1a2b3c4d) which should have a value of 0.0.0.0/0 for its destination.

Hence, the correct answer is: Change the destination of the IGW to 0.0.0.0/0.

The option that says: Changing the destination of the local target to 0.0.0.0/0 is incorrect because the local target, which pertains to your VPC, is already correct. You have to primarily change the IGW instead of the local target entry.

The option that says: Attaching an Elastic IP to the instance is incorrect. Even if you have an Elastic IP, the issue will still persist since your Internet Gateway entry in the route table is incorrect.

The option that says: Attaching a NAT gateway to the VPC is incorrect because the NAT gateway is typically used for EC2 instances hosted in a private subnet to communicate with the Internet.

questions.question:
A company hosted a real estate listing website on 4 On-Demand Amazon EC2 instances, with an Application Load Balancer in front that evenly distributes incoming traffic, and a MySQL RDS instance. Due to the recent TV and social media advertisements, the average response time for the website visitors has significantly increased, as well as the CPU Usage of the EC2 instances. Upon further review, the CloudOps Engineer noted that the CPU Usage of the EC2 instances peaks at 90%, while the database CPU usage is 20%, with an average of 3000 database operations.

Which two options could improve response times? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: EC2 CPU usage is at 90% while RDS usage is low — what two changes improve response times?
choices: Configure an Auto Scaling group for the EC2 instances based on a CPU load threshold.
Upgrade to a Multi-AZ Amazon RDS instance with Read Replicas.
Switch to a different EC2 instance type that has a greater CPU/memory ratio.
Configure an Auto Scaling group for the EC2 instances based on a memory load threshold.
Provision the EC2 instance with a higher number of allowed open TCP connections.
correctAnswers: Switch to a different EC2 instance type that has a greater CPU/memory ratio.
Configure an Auto Scaling group for the EC2 instances based on a CPU load threshold.
questions.explanation:
Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling helps you ensure that you have the correct number of Amazon EC2 instances available to handle the load for your application. You create collections of EC2 instances called Auto Scaling groups. You can specify the minimum number of instances in each Auto Scaling group, and Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling ensures that your group never goes below this size.

You can specify the maximum number of instances in each Auto Scaling group, and Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling ensures that your group never goes above this size. If you specify the desired capacity, either when you create the group or at any time thereafter, Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling ensures that your group has this many instances. If you specify scaling policies, then Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling can launch or terminate instances as demand for your application increases or decreases.

Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling

You can upgrade your EC2 instance to a higher type and configure an Auto Scaling group. You can set up a policy that uses the Amazon CloudWatch CPU Utilization metric to scale up or scale down the number of EC2 instances.

Hence, the correct answers are:

– Switch to a different EC2 instance type that has a greater CPU/memory ratio.

– Configure an Auto Scaling group for the EC2 instances based on a CPU load threshold.

The option that says: Provision the EC2 instance with a higher number of allowed open TCP connections is incorrect because increasing the number of open TCP connections for an instance does not significantly improve the network bandwidth. You typically need to upgrade your instance to a higher EC2 instance type.

The option that says: Configure an Auto Scaling group for the EC2 instances based on a memory load threshold is incorrect, as there is no available metric in CloudWatch that checks the memory load threshold of an instance. This is a custom metric that you have to set up.

The option that says: Upgrade to a Multi-AZ Amazon RDS instance with Read Replicas is incorrect because the scenario primarily indicates that the CPU usage of the database is low and stable. Hence, you don’t need to upgrade your RDS instance at all.

questions.question:
A CloudOps Engineer is managing a web application that uses Amazon ElastiCache for Memcached to cache frequently accessed data. The customers have been complaining about the system performance for the past 3 months. The Engineer monitors the metrics of each service using Amazon CloudWatch. Upon checking, the metrics of the EC2 instances of the web tier are normal but the eviction count has exceeded its threshold.

What should the Engineer do to rectify this issue?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: ElastiCache for Memcached eviction count is too high — what is the fix?
choices: Scale the cluster by increasing the number of read replicas.
Scale the web application by provisioning new EC2 instances.
Scale the cluster by using online resharding.
Scale the cluster by increasing the number of nodes.
correctAnswers: Scale the cluster by increasing the number of nodes.
questions.explanation:
Amazon ElastiCache for Memcached is a great choice for implementing an in-memory cache to decrease access latency, increase throughput, and ease the load off your relational or NoSQL database. Amazon ElastiCache can serve frequently requested items at sub-millisecond response times and enables you to easily scale for higher loads without growing the costlier backend database layer. Database query results caching, persistent session caching, and full-page caching are all popular caching examples with ElastiCache for Memcached.

Caching with ElastiCache for Memcached

Evictions occur when memory is overfilled or greater than the max memory setting in the cache. If the eviction count has exceeded the chosen threshold, you need to scale the cluster up using a larger node type or scale out by adding more nodes to accommodate the additional data. If the eviction rate is steady and your cache hit rate isn’t abnormal, then your cache probably has enough memory. But if the number of evictions increases, you should add more nodes to your existing Memcached Cluster.

Hence, the correct answer is: Scale the cluster by increasing the number of nodes.

The option that says: Scale the cluster by increasing the number of read replicas is incorrect because this option is only applicable in Amazon ElastiCache for Redis. Remember that the web application uses Amazon Elasticache for Memcached. Redis cluster uses read replicas to increase the cluster’s read throughput and to serve high-volume applications’ read traffic.

The option that says: Scale the web application by provisioning new EC2 instances is incorrect because the scenario already stated that the EC2 instances’ metrics of the web tier are normal. Instead of provisioning new EC2 instances for your web application, you should scale up the Amazon ElastiCache cluster by adding more nodes to accommodate the additional data.

The option that says: Scale the cluster by using online resharding is incorrect because this is not applicable in Amazon ElastiCache for Memcached. Online resharding and shard rebalancing allow you to scale your Redis cluster dynamically with no downtime and serve requests even while scaling or rebalancing.

questions.question:
A software development company allows its 75 developers to create and manage resources using a Developer AWS Account. The finance team notices a significant spending increase in the account. The CloudOps Engineer is assigned to collect information about the service costs of each developer to optimize costs.

How can the CloudOps Engineer achieve this requirement? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What two methods help track and analyze AWS resource costs per individual developer?
choices: Create Amazon CloudWatch dashboards to analyze the usage of each developer.
Enable the createdBy tag in the Billing and Management console.
Utilize Budget Alerts to view cost allocation for each developer.
Use Cost Explorer to gain insight into the resources created by developers.
Utilize Trusted Advisor to track the resources created by an IAM User.
correctAnswers: Enable the createdBy tag in the Billing and Management console.
Use Cost Explorer to gain insight into the resources created by developers.
questions.explanation:
The AWS-generated tag createdBy defines and applies to supported AWS resources for cost allocation purposes. To use the AWS-generated tags, a management account owner must activate it in the Billing and Cost Management console. When a management account owner activates the tag, it is also activated for all member accounts.

After the tag is activated, AWS starts applying the tag to resources that are created after the AWS-generated tag was activated. The AWS-generated tags are available only in the Billing and Cost Management console and reports, and doesn’t appear anywhere else in the AWS console, including the AWS Tag Editor. The createdBy tag does not count towards your tags per resource limit.

Cost Explorer is a tool that enables you to view and analyze your costs and usage. You can explore your usage and costs using the main graph, the Cost Explorer cost and usage reports, or the Cost Explorer RI reports.

You can view data for up to the last 12 months, forecast how much you’re likely to spend for the next 12 months, and get recommendations for what Reserved Instances to purchase. You can use Cost Explorer to identify areas that need further inquiry and see trends that you can use to understand your costs.

Hence, the correct answers are:

– Enable the createdBy tag in the Billing and Management console.

– Use Cost Explorer to gain insight into the resources created by developers

The option that says: Utilize Budget Alerts to view cost allocation for each developer is incorrect because this just allows you to get alerts on your designated channels based on your approved AWS portfolios and products.

The option that says: Utilize Trusted Advisor to track the resources created by an IAM User is incorrect because this service does not give you granular reports on spending of each AWS account. Even though it suggests how you can optimize your AWS service costs, it only gives you suggestions on which unused or idle resources can be eliminated, or by making commitments to reserved capacity.

The option that says: Create Amazon CloudWatch dashboards to analyze the usage of each developer is incorrect because Amazon CloudWatch dashboards are primarily used for monitoring service metrics and not for monitoring costs.

questions.question:
An Augmented Reality (AR) gaming company currently has three VPC’s in the us-east-1 region namely: VPC Alpha, VPC Bravo, and VPC Charlie. You are instructed to ensure that all Reserved EC2 instances in all VPC’s can communicate with each other.

Which of the following options should you implement to satisfy the given requirement? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What two requirements must be met to allow full communication between three VPCs using VPC peering?
choices: Ensure that the three VPCs do not have matching or overlapping IPv4 CIDR blocks.
Set up a Transitive VPC Peering for all three VPCs.
Set up VPC Peering for all three VPCs with Edge to Edge Routing.
Set up a VPC Peering with a 'full mesh' configuration for all three VPCs.
Set up a VPC Peering with a 'flying V' configuration for all three VPCs in which you have a VPC peering connection between VPC Alpha and VPC Bravo, and between VPC Alpha and VPC Charlie.
correctAnswers: Set up a VPC Peering with a 'full mesh' configuration for all three VPCs.
Ensure that the three VPCs do not have matching or overlapping IPv4 CIDR blocks.
questions.explanation:
You may want to use a full mesh configuration when you have separate VPCs that need to share resources with each other without restrictions. In this setup, you have to peer the three VPCs together in which VPC Alpha has peered to VPC Bravo; VPC Alpha has peered to VPC Charlie and lastly, VPC Bravo has peered to VPC Charlie.

You cannot create a VPC peering connection between VPCs that have matching or overlapping IPv4 or IPv6 CIDR blocks. Amazon always assigns your VPC a unique IPv6 CIDR block. If your IPv6 CIDR blocks are unique but your IPv4 blocks are not, you cannot create the peering connection.

Hence the correct answers are:

– Set up a VPC Peering with a ‘full mesh’ configuration for all three VPCs.

– Ensure that the three VPCs do not have matching or overlapping IPv4 CIDR blocks.

The option that says: Set up a VPC Peering with a ‘flying V’ configuration for all three VPCs in which you have a VPC peering connection between VPC Alpha and VPC Bravo and between VPC Alpha and VPC Charlie is incorrect. A ‘flying V’ configuration only connects VPC Alpha with VPC Bravo and VPC Alpha with VPC Charlie. This means that there is no connection between VPC Bravo and VPC Charlie hence, the EC2 instances in these two VPC’s would not be able to communicate.

The following options are incorrect because Transitive Peering and Edge to Edge Routing are not supported in VPC Peering.

– Set up a Transitive VPC Peering for all three VPCs.

– Set up VPC Peering for all three VPCs with Edge to Edge Routing.

questions.question:
A SysOps Administrator launched an EBS-backed On-Demand EC2 Instance to host a web application. However, the instance always terminates after going into the pending state.

Which of the following could be the cause of this issue? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What two causes make an EBS-backed EC2 instance immediately terminate from the pending state?
choices: The limit for EC2 Instances in your region has already been reached.
The EBS volume limit has been reached.
The AMI used is corrupted.
The root EBS volume is encrypted and you do not have permission to access the KMS key for decryption.
AWS does not currently have enough available On-Demand capacity to service your request.
correctAnswers: The EBS volume limit has been reached.
The root EBS volume is encrypted and you do not have permission to access the KMS key for decryption.
questions.explanation:
Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) provides scalable computing capacity in the Amazon Web Services (AWS) Cloud. Using Amazon EC2 eliminates your need to invest in hardware upfront, so you can develop and deploy applications faster. You can use Amazon EC2 to launch as many or as few virtual servers as you need, configure security and networking, and manage storage. Amazon EC2 enables you to scale up or down to handle changes in requirements or spikes in popularity, reducing your need to forecast traffic.

The following are a few reasons why your EC2 instance goes from the pending state to the terminated state immediately after restarting it:

– You’ve reached your EBS volume limit.

– An EBS snapshot is corrupt.

– The root EBS volume is encrypted and you do not have permission to access the KMS key for decryption.

– The instance store-backed AMI that you used to launch the instance is missing a required part (an image.part.xx file).

Hence, the correct answers are:

– The EBS volume limit has been reached.

– The root EBS volume is encrypted and you do not have permission to access the KMS key for decryption.

The option that says: The limit for EC2 Instances in your region has already been reached is incorrect because it would affect the launch of new EC2 instances in general, not just this specific instance. If you have reached the maximum number of EC2 instances allowed for your AWS account in a specific region, you would not be able to launch any new EC2 instances until you terminate some existing instances.

The option that says: The AMI used is corrupted is incorrect because if this was the issue then you won’t be able to launch an instance.

The option that says: AWS does not currently have enough available On-Demand capacity to service your request is incorrect because if this was the case, AWS usually provides a message indicating the capacity issue. The message would indicate that the request cannot be fulfilled due to capacity constraints, and you would have to try again later or launch the instance in a different availability zone.

questions.question:
A global company uses AWS Systems Manager Session Manager to manage several Amazon EC2 instances. Each instance is tagged based on the operating system it runs on. The company regularly runs Patch Manager scans to monitor patch compliance across its fleet.

A newly identified zero-day vulnerability, for which no patches are currently available, prompts the company’s security team to create a patch for all affected operating systems. The CloudOps Engineer is tasked with deploying this patch onto the EC2 instances and producing a report confirming the successful execution on each EC2 instance.

Which action must the CloudOps Engineer take to meet these requirements as soon as possible?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What is the fastest way to deploy a custom security patch to all tagged EC2 instances and confirm execution?
choices: Manage the instances using AWS CloudFormation. Create an Amazon S3 bucket from the CloudFormation template. In the launch template properties, include UserData. Add the patch from the security team and command to save logs to the newly created S3 bucket in the UserData. Deploy the stack.

Create a custom automation runbook for AWS Systems Manager Automation. The runbook will have a series of actions to run the security team's patch and save the command history to an Amazon S3 bucket. Execute the automation using the created runbook.

Create an AWS Lambda function that connects to the EC2 instances through SSH. Configure Lambda to check the operating system based on the tag, run the security team's patch, and save the result to an Amazon S3 bucket. Query the results using Amazon Athena.

Utilize Systems Manager Run Command and opt for either the AWS-RunShellScript document or the AWS-RunPowerShellScript document. Configure Run Command with the security team's patch. In the Targets parameter, enter the operating system tag. Provide the security team with evidence from the command history.
correctAnswers: Utilize Systems Manager Run Command and opt for either the AWS-RunShellScript document or the AWS-RunPowerShellScript document. Configure Run Command with the security team's patch. In the Targets parameter, enter the operating system tag. Provide the security team with evidence from the command history.
questions.explanation:
Using Run Command, a capability of AWS Systems Manager, you can remotely and securely manage the configuration of your managed nodes. A managed node is any Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (Amazon EC2) instance or non-EC2 machine in your hybrid and multicloud environment that has been configured for Systems Manager. Run Command allows you to automate common administrative tasks and perform one-time configuration changes at scale.

Before you can manage nodes by using Run Command, a capability of AWS Systems Manager, configure an AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) policy for any user who will run commands. You must also configure your nodes for Systems Manager.

Using Run Command and the AWS-RunShellScript document for Linux and AWS-RunPowerShellScript for Windows, you can run any command or script on a managed node as if you were logged on locally.

Run Command

You can select which instances you want to run the command by choosing the managed nodes on which you want to run this operation by specifying tags, selecting instances or edge devices manually, or specifying a resource group.

With this, you can deploy the security team’s code to all EC2 instances. This code should mitigate the risk of the zero-day vulnerability. A zero-day vulnerability is a software vulnerability discovered by attackers before the vendor even knows it exists. Therefore, there is still no patch available.
