User traffic is carried from the edge locations to the closest regional application endpoint over the AWS Global network, thereby reducing the impact of internet weather on user traffic and improving customer experience. Global Accelerator continually monitors the health of application endpoints and routes the traffic to the optimal endpoint based on health, client location, and configured policies. In the event of health check failures, Global Accelerator automatically reroutes end-user traffic to healthy endpoints.

You can configure a standard accelerator to utilize the performance improvement of the application due to automatic routing to the nearest regional application endpoint feature and the automatic failover feature of the AWS Global accelerator. The Global Accelerator also provides static IP addresses that will satisfy the requirement for third-party services.

Hence, the correct answer is: Configure an AWS Global Accelerator, standard accelerator type. Add listeners. Add an endpoint group for each region. Add endpoints associated with specific endpoint groups.

The option that says: Configure A records in Route 53 with identical names for each endpoint. Select latency routing policy. Configure a health check for each endpoint and associate with each record is incorrect. While this approach will let the user’s requests be routed based on latency, it is still inadequate as it is not capable of having static IP addresses. If your application is hosted in multiple AWS Regions, you can improve performance for your users by serving their requests from the AWS Region that provides the lowest latency. Configuring health checks will also enable automatic failover to healthy endpoints. However, this solution cannot satisfy the requirement for third-party services to have static IP addresses. Latency-based routing relies on DNS resolution, and IP addresses associated with endpoints can change dynamically.

The option that says: Configure A records in Route 53 with identical names for each endpoint. Select geoproximity routing policy. Configure a health check for each endpoint and associate with each record is incorrect because the geoproximity routing policy cannot route the users based on latency. Geoproximity routing policy is primarily used when you want to route traffic based on the location of your resources or shift traffic flow between your resources.

The option that says: Configure A records in Route 53 with identical names for each endpoint. Select failover routing policy. Configure a health check for each endpoint and associate with each record is incorrect. Even though it is true that this solution can provide a failover capability, it still cannot satisfy the requirement for routing based on latency. Failover routing policy allows you to direct users to a particular resource only when the resource is healthy as the primary and provide a backup resource as secondary when the primary resource is unhealthy. This solution cannot redirect the users to multiple regions based on latency.

questions.question:
A test site runs on a group of Amazon EC2 instances in us-east-1 on a VPC. The application needs to have an incoming and outgoing connection to the internet.

Which steps should a CloudOps Engineer perform to provide internet connectivity to the EC2 instances in the us-east-1 region? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What two steps are required to give EC2 instances in a VPC both inbound and outbound internet access?
choices: Create an Internet Gateway and attach it to the VPC in us-east-1.
Associate an Elastic IP to the internet gateway.
Provision a NAT gateway to a public subnet in us-east-1.
Create a route entry on the VPC's routing table for the subnet that points to the internet gateway.
Attach a private IP address to the elastic network interfaces of all the EC2 instances in us-east-1.
correctAnswers: Create an Internet Gateway and attach it to the VPC in us-east-1.
Create a route entry on the VPC's routing table for the subnet that points to the internet gateway.
questions.explanation:
To enable access to or from the internet for instances in a subnet in a VPC, you must do the following.

– Create an internet gateway and attach it to your VPC.

– Add a route to your subnet’s route table that directs internet-bound traffic to the internet gateway.

– Ensure that instances in your subnet have a globally unique IP address (public IPv4 address, Elastic IP address, or IPv6 address).

– Ensure that your network access control lists and security group rules allow the relevant traffic to flow to and from your instance.

Hence, the correct answers are:

– Create an Internet Gateway and attach it to the VPC in us-east-1.

– Create a route entry on the VPC’s routing table for the subnet that points to the internet gateway.

The option that says: Provision a NAT gateway to a public subnet in us-east-1 is incorrect because NAT gateways are mainly used for connecting private subnets to services outside your VPC. This does not allow external services to initiate a connection to instances inside your VPC.

The option that says: Associate an Elastic IP to the internet gateway is incorrect because you can’t associate an Elastic IP to an internet gateway. EIPs can only be associated with an instance or a network interface.

The option that says: Attach a private IP address to the elastic network interfaces of all the EC2 instances in us-east-1 is incorrect because attaching a private IP address on EC2 instances does not do anything to allow an instance to connect to the internet.

questions.question:
A CloudOps Engineer needs to produce regular reports and statistics on Amazon EC2 resource consumption across different regions. In an upcoming meeting, the Engineer is asked to present these findings to the CTO and the Data Analytics team. Aggregating these statistics would provide a comprehensive view of resource consumption.

What is the procedure for viewing aggregation statistics in Amazon CloudWatch?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you aggregate and view CloudWatch metrics across multiple AWS regions?
choices: There is no way to view aggregate statistics in CloudWatch. CloudWatch cannot aggregate data across Regions.
Create an AWS Lambda function that collects metrics from your EC2 instances, computes your desired statistical output, and sends it to CloudWatch for viewing.
Use CloudWatch Metric Math to query metrics and apply mathematical operations on these metrics.
Enable detailed monitoring on your EC2 instances.
correctAnswers: Use CloudWatch Metric Math to query metrics and apply mathematical operations on these metrics.
questions.explanation:
Amazon CloudWatch now includes cross-account cross-region dashboards, which enable you to create high-level operational dashboards, and with one click, drill down into more specific dashboards in different AWS accounts without having to log in and out of different accounts or switch AWS Regions. It is intended for centralized operations teams, DevOps engineers, and service owners who need to monitor, troubleshoot, and analyze applications running in multiple regions and accounts. The ability to visualize, aggregate, and summarize performance and operational data across accounts and Regions help reduce mean time to resolution.

Amazon CloudWatch

With cross-account cross-region dashboards, you get centralized visibility of CloudWatch metrics, logs, and alarms across a group of related accounts without the overhead of having to centralize data. Likewise, you can visualize the health and performance of applications running in several Regions.

You can also use CloudWatch metric math to aggregate and transform metrics from multiple accounts and Regions. Metric math enables you to query multiple CloudWatch metrics and use math expressions to create new time series based on these metrics. You can visualize the resulting time series on the CloudWatch console and add them to dashboards. Using AWS Lambda metrics as an example, you could divide the Errors metric by the Invocations metric to get an error rate. Then add the resulting time series to a graph on your CloudWatch dashboard.

Hence, the correct answer is: Use CloudWatch Metric Math to query metrics and apply mathematical operations on these metrics.

The option that says: Enable detailed monitoring on your EC2 instances is incorrect because detailed monitoring simply increases the frequency of tracking the metrics to a 1-minute period.

The option that says: Create an AWS Lambda function that collects metrics from your EC2 instances, computes your desired statistical output, and sends it to CloudWatch for viewing is incorrect because you don’t need a Lambda function to fetch metrics from your EC2 instances. CloudWatch can typically do this without using Lambda.

The option that says: There is no way to view aggregate statistics in CloudWatch. CloudWatch cannot aggregate data across Regions is incorrect because this is actually possible with CloudWatch cross-account cross-region dashboards.

questions.question:
A startup hosted a photo editing application on a large On-Demand Amazon EC2 instance. The application allows users to add effects and filters to photos, which are then stored as a media file in an Amazon S3 bucket. The architecture should use S3 Event Notifications to trigger asynchronous processing whenever a new photo is uploaded.

Which of the following services will help build a scalable and decoupled architecture in AWS for this application?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which AWS service decouples S3 event-driven workloads to enable scalable asynchronous processing?
choices: AWS STS
S3 Glacier
AWS Elastic Transcoder
Amazon SQS
correctAnswers: Amazon SQS
questions.explanation:
Amazon Simple Queue Service (SQS) offers a reliable, highly scalable hosted queue for storing messages as they travel between applications or microservices. It moves data between distributed application components and helps you decouple these components. Amazon SQS provides common middleware constructs such as dead-letter queues and poison-pill management.

How Amazon SQS Works

Amazon SQS lets you easily decouple the components of a cloud application. You can use Amazon SQS to transmit any volume of data without losing messages or requiring other services to be always available. Using SQS in this scenario, the data can be temporarily saved on the SQS queue while the EC2 instances pull the data from the queue and process it. This setup is highly scalable.

Hence, the correct answer is: Amazon SQS.

S3 Glacier is incorrect because Glacier is a service that is used as a low-cost storage solution suitable for archiving data.

AWS Elastic Transcoder is incorrect because Elastic Transcoder is used for transcoding video files into different formats.

AWS STS is incorrect because STS is a web service that is used for requesting temporary tokens to authenticate users.

questions.question:
A global news website is deployed in AWS, which uses an Application Load Balancer, an Auto Scaling group of On-Demand Amazon EC2 instances, and an Amazon RDS MySQL Database. Lately, there are a lot of readers who are complaining about the slow loading of the articles on the website. As the CloudOps Engineer of the company, it is required to analyze the current architecture using Performance Insights, and it has been found that there is a high number of read operations in the database, which affects the website’s performance.

Which of the following options would be considered to resolve the issue in a cost-effective manner?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What is the most cost-effective way to reduce high read load on an RDS MySQL database?
choices: Launch a large Amazon ElastiCache instance to work as a database cache for RDS.
Change the RDS instance to use Multi-AZ deployments.
Use Read Replicas.
Use the AWS Database Migration Service (AWS DMS) to automatically migrate the database to an Amazon Aurora instance.
correctAnswers: Use Read Replicas.
questions.explanation:
Amazon RDS Read Replicas provide enhanced performance and durability for database (DB) instances. This feature makes it easy to elastically scale out beyond the capacity constraints of a single DB instance for read-heavy database workloads. You can create one or more replicas of a given source DB Instance and serve high-volume application read traffic from multiple copies of your data, thereby increasing aggregate read throughput. Read replicas can also be promoted when needed to become standalone DB instances. Read replicas are available in Amazon RDS for MySQL, MariaDB, Oracle, and PostgreSQL, as well as Amazon Aurora.

Table comparing AWS Multi AZ, Multi Region, and Read replicas.

Hence, the correct answer is: Use Read Replicas.

The option that says: Launch a large Amazon ElastiCache instance to work as a database cache for RDS is incorrect because it is simply expensive compared to Read Replicas.

The option that says: Change the RDS instance to use Multi-AZ deployments is incorrect because Multi-AZ deployments are primarily used to provide high availability to the database.

The option that says: Use the AWS Database Migration Service (AWS DMS) to automatically migrate the database to an Amazon Aurora instance is incorrect because Aurora costs more than RDS and the migration does not solve high read load. Read Replicas are cheaper and directly offload read operations without database changes.

questions.question:
A company wants to track and review the usage of its resources using user-defined cost allocation tags due to an exorbitant AWS bill that it received recently. The CloudOps Engineer needs to view the costs for the current month as well as the last three months. The engineer also needs to be able to forecast expenses for the current billing period.

Which of the following AWS Cost Management tools should the Administrator use?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which AWS tool lets you view historical costs by tag and forecast upcoming expenses?
choices: AWS Budgets
AWS Cost and Usage report
AWS Cost Explorer
Amazon QuickSight
correctAnswers: AWS Cost Explorer
questions.explanation:
Cost Explorer is a tool that enables you to view and analyze your costs and usage. You can explore your usage and costs using the main graph, the Cost Explorer cost and usage reports, or the Cost Explorer RI reports. You can view data for up to the last 13 months, forecast how much you’re likely to spend for the next three months, and get recommendations for what Reserved Instances to purchase. You can use Cost Explorer to identify areas that need further inquiry and see trends that you can use to understand your costs.a

Cost Explorer is a tool that enables you to view and analyze your costs and usage. You can explore your usage and costs using the main graph, the Cost Explorer cost and usage reports, or the Cost Explorer RI reports. You can view data for up to the last 13 months, forecast how much you’re likely to spend for the next three months, and get recommendations for what Reserved Instances to purchase. You can use Cost Explorer to identify areas that need further inquiry and see trends that you can use to understand your costs.

A forecast is a prediction of how much you will use AWS services over the forecast time period that you selected, based on your past usage. Forecasting provides an estimate of what your AWS bill will be and enables you to use alarms and budgets for amounts that you’re predicted to use. Because forecasts are predictions, the forecasted billing amounts are estimated and might differ from your actual charges for each statement period.

Forecast

When you first sign up for Cost Explorer, AWS prepares the data about your costs for the current month and the last three months and then calculates the forecast for the next three months. The current month’s data is available for viewing in about 24 hours. The rest of your data takes a few days longer. Cost Explorer updates your cost data at least once every 24 hours. After you sign up, Cost Explorer can display up to 12 months of historical data (if you have that much), the current month, and the forecasted costs for the next three months. The first time that you use Cost Explorer, Cost Explorer walks you through the main parts of the console with an explanation for each section. You can trigger this walkthrough at a later time as well.

Hence, the correct answer is: AWS Cost Explorer.

Amazon QuickSight is incorrect because this is primarily a fully managed service that lets you easily create and publish interactive dashboards. It is not suitable for viewing your current AWS bill, nor forecasting future costs and usage of your AWS resources based on your past consumption.

AWS Cost and Usage report is incorrect because this tool doesn’t forecast your future costs. It simply lists the AWS usage for each service category used by an account and its IAM users in hourly or daily line items, as well as any tags that you have activated for cost allocation purposes.

AWS Budgets is incorrect. Although this service enables you to set custom cost and usage budgets and receive alerts when your usage exceeds the thresholds, it does not offer the same historical cost analysis or forecasting tools as Cost Explorer.

questions.question:
An image hosting company sets up an infrastructure in the us-east-2 region where EC2 instances store and request photos from an S3 bucket. The senior CloudOps Engineer is required to restrict access to the S3 bucket to Amazon EC2 instances in a single VPC to secure the infrastructure. He also needs to ensure that all network traffic flows over the AWS private network only.

What solution should the CloudOps Engineer implement to achieve the requirement?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you restrict S3 bucket access to a specific VPC and route all traffic over the AWS private network?
choices: Build a VPC endpoint to connect your EC2 instance and S3 bucket. Then, configure an IAM policy to conditionally limit all S3 actions on the bucket when the source traffic is from the VPC endpoint.
Build a NAT gateway. Alter the routing table to forward incoming traffic destined for the Amazon S3 bucket to the NAT gateway.

Provision a VPC endpoint to allow your EC2 instances and S3 bucket to communicate. Configure the S3 bucket's policy to conditionally limit all S3 actions on the bucket if the request is coming from the IP address of the VPC endpoint.
Create an Amazon EC2 service-linked role that allows instances to communicate directly with Amazon S3. Attach an IAM policy to the service-linked role that allows full access to the bucket.
correctAnswers: Provision a VPC endpoint to allow your EC2 instances and S3 bucket to communicate. Configure the S3 bucket's policy to conditionally limit all S3 actions on the bucket if the request is coming from the IP address of the VPC endpoint.
questions.explanation:
A VPC endpoint enables private connections between your VPC and supported AWS services and VPC endpoint services powered by AWS PrivateLink.

Traffic between your VPC and the other service does not leave the Amazon network. A VPC endpoint does not require an internet gateway, virtual private gateway, NAT device, VPN connection, or AWS Direct Connect connection. Instances in your VPC do not require public IP addresses to communicate with resources in the service.

A bucket policy is a resource-based AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) policy. You add a bucket policy to a bucket to grant other AWS accounts or IAM users access permissions for the bucket and the objects in it. Object permissions apply only to the objects that the bucket owner creates.

There are two key phrases that described the specific requirements in the scenario. The first is “to restrict access to the S3 bucket” and the second one is to “ensure that all network traffic should only flow over the AWS private network”.

To make sure that the network traffic from the EC2 to the S3 bucket is private, you need to set up a VPC endpoint that will allow your VPC to connect to Amazon S3. Moreover, a bucket policy will help you restrict access to the bucket. When creating the policy, you can create a condition to allow access if the request is coming from the IP address of the VPC endpoint.

Hence, the correct answer is: Provision a VPC endpoint to allow your EC2 instances and S3 bucket to communicate. Configure the S3 bucket’s policy to conditionally limit all S3 actions on the bucket if the request is coming from the IP address of the VPC endpoint.

The option that says: Build a VPC endpoint to connect your EC2 instance and S3 bucket. Then, configure an IAM policy to conditionally limit all S3 actions on the bucket when the source traffic is from the VPC endpoint is incorrect. While this can be done, this policy is not enforced on the S3 bucket itself which means that any user with the IAM policy can still access the bucket. The requirement in the scenario is to restrict access to the bucket which makes this option incorrect.

The option that says: Build a NAT gateway. Alter the routing table to forward incoming traffic destined for the Amazon S3 bucket to the NAT gateway is incorrect because A NAT gateway is a Network Address Translation (NAT) service and will not help you route traffic to Amazon S3 through a private network.

The option that says: Create an Amazon EC2 service-linked role that allows instances to communicate directly with Amazon S3. Attach an IAM policy to the service-linked role that allows full access to the bucket is incorrect because a service-linked role is mainly used to provide a secure way of delegating permissions to AWS services. In addition, this will not help in ensuring that the EC2 instances will communicate via a private network.

questions.question:
A global technology company has thousands of employees around the globe that are using Amazon VPC Cloud. As part of the company’s security compliance, IT auditors have requested a Credential report which contains a list of AWS users that contains their current status, their access key usage, and if they are using Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) or not.

How can the CloudOps Engineer generate the report required by the auditors?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Where do you generate an IAM credential report showing user MFA status and access key usage?
choices: You can call up AWS support and have them generate the Credential report for you.
You can go to AWS IAM Console and download the Credential report.
You can contact an AWS partner to generate the Credential report.
Go to AWS EC2 dashboard and download the Credential report.
correctAnswers: You can go to AWS IAM Console and download the Credential report.
questions.explanation:
AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) enables you to manage access to AWS services and resources securely. Using IAM, you can create and manage AWS users and groups, and use permissions to allow and deny their access to AWS resources.

You can generate and download a credential report that lists all users in your account and the status of their various credentials, including passwords, access keys, and MFA devices. You can get this credential report from the AWS Management Console, the AWS SDKs and Command Line Tools, or the IAM API.

Hence, the correct answer is: Go to AWS IAM Console and download the Credential report.

The options that say: Call up AWS support and have them generate the Credential report for you and Contact an AWS partner to generate the Credential report are both incorrect since the IAM section already provides the report for you.

The option that says: Go to AWS EC2 dashboard and downloading the Credential report is incorrect because there is no option for this on the EC2 dashboard.

questions.question:
A leading bank has a web application that is heavily using the Amazon RDS instance for its database tier. The CloudOps Engineer is instructed to monitor how the different processes or threads on a DB instance use the CPU, such as the percentage of the CPU bandwidth and total memory consumed by each process.

Which of the following is the most suitable solution to properly monitor the database?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you monitor per-process CPU and memory usage on an RDS DB instance in real time?
choices: Write a shell script that collects and publishes custom metrics to Amazon CloudWatch, which tracks the real-time CPU Utilization of the RDS instance.
View the CPU% and MEM% metrics, which are readily available in the RDS console.
Set up a monitoring system that uses Amazon CloudWatch to track the CPU Utilization of your database.
Enable Enhanced Monitoring in RDS.
correctAnswers: Enable Enhanced Monitoring in RDS.
questions.explanation:
Amazon RDS provides metrics in real time for the operating system (OS) that your DB instance runs on. You can view the metrics for your DB instance using the console, or consume the Enhanced Monitoring JSON output from CloudWatch Logs in a monitoring system of your choice. By default, Enhanced Monitoring metrics are stored in the CloudWatch Logs for 30 days. To modify the amount of time the metrics are stored in the CloudWatch Logs, change the retention for the RDSOSMetrics log group in the CloudWatch console.

Process List

Take note that there are certain differences between CloudWatch and Enhanced Monitoring Metrics. CloudWatch gathers metrics about CPU utilization from the hypervisor for a DB instance, and Enhanced Monitoring gathers its metrics from an agent on the instance. As a result, you might find differences between the measurements, because the hypervisor layer performs a small amount of work.

The differences can be greater if your DB instances use smaller instance classes because then there are likely more virtual machines (VMs) that are managed by the hypervisor layer on a single physical instance. Enhanced Monitoring metrics are useful when you want to see how different processes or threads on a DB instance use the CPU.

Hence, the correct answer is: Enable Enhanced Monitoring in RDS.

The option that says: View the CPU% and MEM% metrics, which are readily available in the RDS console is incorrect because the CPU% and MEM% metrics are not readily available in the Amazon RDS console, which is contrary to what is being stated in this option.

The option that says: Write a shell script that collects and publishes custom metrics to Amazon CloudWatch, which tracks the real-time CPU Utilization of the RDS instance is incorrect. Although you can typically use Amazon CloudWatch Logs and CloudWatch dashboard to monitor the CPU Utilization of the database instance, using CloudWatch alone is still not enough to get the specific percentage of the CPU bandwidth and total memory consumed by each database processes. The data provided by CloudWatch is not as detailed as compared with the Enhanced Monitoring feature in RDS. Take note as well that you do not have direct access to the instances/servers of your RDS database instance, unlike with your EC2 instances, where you can install a CloudWatch agent or a custom script to get CPU and memory utilization of your instance.

The option that says: Set up a monitoring system that uses Amazon CloudWatch to track the CPU Utilization of your database is incorrect. Although you can primarily use Amazon CloudWatch to monitor the CPU Utilization of your database instance, it does not provide the percentage of the CPU bandwidth and total memory consumed by each database process in your RDS instance. Take note that CloudWatch gathers metrics about CPU utilization from the hypervisor for a DB instance, while RDS Enhanced Monitoring gathers its metrics from an agent on the instance.

questions.question:
A finance organization is running an application on its on-premises data center but needs to use AWS for backing up its data. The manager requires that the backup data is made available locally to comply with regulatory requirements. The backup software can only write to block-based storage compatible with Portable Operating System Interface (POSIX).

Which backup solution will help the CloudOps Engineer meet the requirements?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which AWS Storage Gateway mode stores primary data locally while asynchronously backing it up to AWS?
choices: Utilize AWS Storage Gateway and configure it to operate using stored volumes.
Utilize AWS Storage Gateway and configure it to use cached volumes.
Configure the backup software to target an Amazon S3 bucket for the data backups.
Configure the backup software to target Amazon S3 Glacier when backing up data.
correctAnswers: Utilize AWS Storage Gateway and configure it to operate using stored volumes.
questions.explanation:
Volume Gateway presents cloud-backed iSCSI block storage volumes to your on-premises applications. Volume Gateway stores and manages on-premises data in Amazon S3 on your behalf and operates in either cache mode or stored mode.

In the cached Volume Gateway mode, your primary data is stored in Amazon S3, while retaining your frequently accessed data locally in the cache for low latency access. In the stored Volume Gateway mode, your primary data is stored locally and your entire dataset is available for low latency access on premises while also asynchronously getting backed up to Amazon S3.

Since it is required that the backup data should also be stored locally aside from backing it up on AWS, the best solution is to use AWS Storage Gateway that operates in stored mode.

Hence, the correct answer is: Utilize AWS Storage Gateway and configure it to operate using stored volumes.

The option that says: Utilize AWS Storage Gateway and configure it to use cached volumes is incorrect because cached volumes are only used when you want to store your primary data in Amazon S3 while retaining your frequently accessed data locally in the cache for low latency access.

The option that says: Configure the backup software to target an Amazon S3 bucket for the data backups is incorrect because targeting an S3 bucket in AWS will not save the backup data locally. This will not help you keep a copy of backup data on your on-premises data center.

The option that says: Configure the backup software to target Amazon S3 Glacier when backing up data is incorrect because this does not store your backup data locally which does not meet the requirement.

questions.question:
A CloudOps Engineer needs to set up a PostgreSQL database server that runs on a Reserved EC2 instance which will be used by various internal applications within a VPC. To simplify the naming convention of the database server, the Engineer is planning to allocate a custom domain name for the database.

Which of the following should the Engineer do to complete this task?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you assign a custom private DNS name to an EC2-hosted database server accessible only within a VPC?
choices: Set up a private hosted zone in Route 53. Create a CNAME record, such as db.tutorialsdojo.com, and specify the IP address of the database server.
Set up a publicly hosted zone in Route 53. Create a CNAME record, such as db.tutorialsdojo.com, and specify the IP address of the database server.
Set up a private hosted zone in Route 53. Create an A or AAAA record, such as db.tutorialsdojo.com, and specify the IP address of the database server.
Set up a publicly hosted zone in Route 53. Create an A or AAAA record, such as db.tutorialsdojo.com, and specify the IP address of the database server.
correctAnswers: Set up a private hosted zone in Route 53. Create an A or AAAA record, such as db.tutorialsdojo.com, and specify the IP address of the database server.
questions.explanation:
A private hosted zone is a container that holds information about how you want Amazon Route 53 to respond to DNS queries for a domain and its subdomains within one or more VPCs that you create with the Amazon VPC service.

Here’s how private hosted zones work:

1. You create a private hosted zone, such as example.com, and specify the VPCs that you want to associate with the hosted zone.

2. You create records in the hosted zone that determine how Route 53 responds to DNS queries for your domain and subdomains within and among your VPCs. For example, suppose you have a database server that runs on an EC2 instance in one of the VPCs that you associated with your private hosted zone. You create an A or AAAA record, such as db.example.com, and you specify the IP address of the database server.

3. When an application submits a DNS query for db.example.com, Route 53 returns the corresponding IP address. The application must also be running on an EC2 instance in one of the VPCs that you associated with the example.com private hosted zone.

4. The application uses the IP address that it got from Route 53 to establish a connection with the database server.

Hence, the correct answer is: Set up a private hosted zone in Route 53. Create an A or AAAA record, such as db.tutorialsdojo.com, and specify the IP address of the database server.

The option that says: Set up a private hosted zone in Route 53. Create a CNAME record, such as db.tutorialsdojo.com, and specify the IP address of the database server is incorrect because it suggests using a CNAME record, which is not recommended for use with database servers. CNAME records are alias records and they can cause additional DNS lookups and result in performance issues.

The option that says: Set up a publicly hosted zone in Route 53. Create a CNAME record, such as db.tutorialsdojo.com, and specify the IP address of the database server is incorrect because it suggests setting up a publicly hosted zone in Route 53, which is not recommended for internal applications. Public hosted zones are used for internet-facing resources, while private hosted zones are used for internal resources within a VPC.

The option that says: Set up a publicly hosted zone in Route 53. Create an A or AAAA record, such as db.tutorialsdojo.com, and specify the IP address of the database server is incorrect because like the option above, it also suggests using a publicly hosted zone.

questions.question:
An international IT Consulting company is planning on setting up multiple accounts in AWS to separate its various departments and project teams. For security purposes, the IT Security department has a requirement to ensure that certain services and actions are not allowed across all the accounts. While customer managed policies can control permissions within a single account, such policies cannot enforce restrictions across multiple accounts.

How would this be achieved in the most effective way?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: How do you enforce service and action restrictions uniformly across multiple AWS accounts?
choices: Contact AWS and request for them to deny the services not allowed to be used across accounts.
Use AWS Organizations and Service Control Policies to control services on each account.
Activate Consolidated Billing feature across the account to control the services on each account.
Create a common IAM policy that can be applied across all accounts.
correctAnswers: Use AWS Organizations and Service Control Policies to control services on each account.
questions.explanation:
AWS Organizations offers policy-based management for multiple AWS accounts. With Organizations, you can create groups of accounts, automate account creation, and apply and manage policies for those groups. Organizations enable you to centrally manage policies across multiple accounts without requiring custom scripts and manual processes. It allows you to create Service Control Policies (SCPs) that centrally control AWS service use across multiple AWS accounts.

AWS Organizations

Hence, the correct answer is: Use AWS Organizations and Service Control Policies to control services on each account.

The option that says: Create a common IAM policy that can be applied across all accounts is incorrect only because it is not possible to create a common IAM policy.

The option that says: Activate Consolidated Billing feature across the account to control the services on each account is incorrect since Consolidated Billing is just another feature under AWS Organization.

The option that says: Contact AWS and request for them to deny the services not allowed to be used across accounts is incorrect since AWS should not be responsible for denying the usage of services in an account.

questions.question: A facial recognition system is using an Auto Scaling group of On-Demand EC2 instances. It has one specific instance which is causing an error and must be terminated immediately. Using the AWS CLI, how can you terminate an instance from the specified Auto Scaling group without updating the size of the group?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which AWS CLI command terminates a specific Auto Scaling group instance without decreasing the desired capacity?
choices: update-auto-scaling-group --auto-scaling-group-name --terminate --instance-id
terminate-instance-in-auto-scaling-group --instance-id
terminate-instance-in-auto-scaling-group --instance-id --should-decrement-desired-capacity
terminate-instance-in-auto-scaling-group --instance-id --no-should-decrement-desired-capacity
correctAnswers: terminate-instance-in-auto-scaling-group --instance-id --no-should-decrement-desired-capacity
questions.explanation:
The terminate-instance-in-auto-scaling-group CLI command terminates the specified instance and optionally adjusts the desired group size. This call simply makes a termination request so the instance is not terminated immediately.

This command has a required parameter which indicates whether terminating the instance also decrements the size of the Auto Scaling group:

--should-decrement-desired-capacity | --no-should-decrement-desired-capacity (boolean)

The example below terminates the specified instance from the specified Auto Scaling group without updating the size of the group:

aws autoscaling terminate-instance-in-auto-scaling-group --instance-id i-93633f9b --no-should-decrement-desired-capacity

Auto Scaling launches a replacement instance after the specified instance terminates.

Hence, the correct answer is: terminate-instance-in-auto-scaling-group --instance-id --no-should-decrement-desired-capacity.

terminate-instance-in-auto-scaling-group --instance-id is incorrect because the --should-decrement-desired-capacity or --no-should-decrement-desired-capacity parameter is required for the CLI command.

terminate-instance-in-auto-scaling-group --instance-id --should-decrement-desired-capacity is incorrect because this will update the size of the group, which is the opposite of what is needed.

update-auto-scaling-group --auto-scaling-group-name --terminate --instance-id is incorrect because the update-auto-scaling-group CLI command is not applicable for this scenario.

questions.question:
A merchandising company utilizes Amazon Elasticache for Redis to boost the performance of its inventory management application. The Elasticache for Redis cluster includes two extra-large nodes distributed across two Availability Zones. The company’s CloudOps team monitored the metrics for the cluster and found that it still has 80% freeable memory.

What is the MOST cost-effective approach to resize the cluster while maintaining high availability for the application?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What is the most cost-effective way to downsize an over-provisioned ElastiCache for Redis cluster with high availability?
choices: Remove one node from the Elasticache for Redis cluster.
Set up a new Elasticache for Redis cluster that uses large nodes. Migrate the data from the old cluster to the newly created one. Once done, shut down the old cluster.
Set up a new Elasticache for Redis cluster that uses large nodes. Take a backup of the current cluster and restore the backup in the newly created cluster. Once done, delete the old cluster
Change the node types from extra-large to large by performing an online resizing on the ElastiCache for Redis cluster.
correctAnswers: Change the node types from extra-large to large by performing an online resizing on the ElastiCache for Redis cluster.
questions.explanation:
By using online vertical scaling with Amazon ElastiCache for Redis version 3.2.10 or newer, you can scale your Redis clusters dynamically with minimal downtime. This allows your Redis cluster to serve requests even while scaling.

You can do the following:

– Scale up – Increase read and write capacity by adjusting the node type of your Redis cluster to use a larger node type.

ElastiCache dynamically resizes your cluster while remaining online and serving requests.

– Scale down – Reduce read and write capacity by adjusting the node type down to use a smaller node. Again, ElastiCache dynamically resizes your cluster while remaining online and serving requests. In this case, you reduce costs by downsizing the node.

To maintain high availability, you have to make sure that there are at least two nodes in the cluster. Which means there is no need to remove or add a node since it is given in the scenario that there are two nodes already.

With online resizing in Amazon Elasticache for Redis, you won’t have to pay for backups and migration costs, all you have to pay for are the resources provisioned when a scale up or scale out is performed. This makes online resizing the most cost-efficient approach when scaling your clusters.

Hence, the correct answer is: Change the node types from extra-large to large by performing an online resizing on the ElastiCache for Redis cluster.

The option that says: Remove one node from the Elasticache for Redis cluster is incorrect because removing a node will not satisfy the requirement of maintaining high availability for the cluster.

The option that says: Set up a new Elasticache for Redis cluster that uses large nodes. Migrate the data from the old cluster to the newly created one. Once done, shut down the old cluster is incorrect because this is not a cost-efficient solution. Creating a new cluster entails additional costs. Migration will take some time and once it is done, the old cluster is only shut down which will also incur costs if not deleted.

The option that says: Set up a new Elasticache for Redis cluster that uses large nodes. Take a backup of the current cluster and restore the backup in the newly created cluster. Once done, delete the old cluster is incorrect because taking backups and restoring them incur extra costs. Creating a new cluster with large nodes will also give you additional costs which means this is not a cost-efficient solution.

questions.question:
A Cloud Engineer has recently launched an Auto Scaling group of Spot Amazon EC2 instances with an Application Load Balancer. To properly monitor all of the web applications in the VPC, the manager wants to enable the Detailed Monitoring feature in Amazon CloudWatch. This will allow the Engineer to monitor the CPUUtilization, DiskReadBytes, NetworkIn, and other metrics of the instances.

Which of the following statements is true about this feature?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: What is a key characteristic of enabling CloudWatch Detailed Monitoring on EC2 instances?
choices: The CloudWatch data metrics of the Auto Scaling group will be sent every five minutes with no additional charge.
CloudWatch Detailed Monitoring does not support Auto Scaling.
The EC2 instances of the Auto Scaling group will send CloudWatch data metrics every minute with an additional charge.
The EC2 instances of the Auto Scaling group will send CloudWatch data metrics every minute with no additional charge.
correctAnswers: The EC2 instances of the Auto Scaling group will send CloudWatch data metrics every minute with an additional charge.
questions.explanation:
You can monitor your instances using Amazon CloudWatch, which collects and processes raw data from Amazon EC2 into readable, near-real-time metrics. These statistics are recorded for a period of 15 months so that you can access historical information and gain a better perspective on how your web application or service is performing.

By default, Amazon EC2 sends metric data to CloudWatch in 5-minute periods. To send metric data for your instance to CloudWatch in 1-minute periods, you can enable detailed monitoring on the instance.

Take note that there are two types of metrics:

1. Amazon EC2 CloudWatch metrics ( CPUUtilization, DiskReadBytes, NetworkIn, etc..)

2. Auto Scaling Group metrics ( GroupInServiceInstances, GroupPendingInstances, etc…)

Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling publishes data points to CloudWatch about your Auto Scaling groups. The metrics are available at 1-minute granularity at no additional charge, but you must enable them. By doing this, you get continuous visibility into the operations of your Auto Scaling groups so that you can quickly respond to changes in your workloads.

Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling

The Amazon EC2 console displays a series of graphs based on the raw data from Amazon CloudWatch. Depending on your needs, you might prefer to get data for your instances from Amazon CloudWatch instead of the graphs in the console.

CloudWatch Detailed Monitoring supports mostly all AWS services including Auto Scaling and sends data every minute. Once enabled, detailed monitoring incurs an additional cost.

Hence, the correct answer is: The EC2 instances of the Auto Scaling group will send CloudWatch data metrics every minute with an additional charge.

The option that says: CloudWatch Detailed Monitoring does not support Auto Scaling is incorrect because CloudWatch Detailed Monitoring primarily supports Auto Scaling.

The option that says: The EC2 instances of the Auto Scaling group will send CloudWatch data metrics every minute with no additional charge is incorrect because there is an additional charge for Detailed Monitoring. Basic monitoring is the default setting and has no additional charge. The Auto Scaling Group metrics are available at 1-minute granularity at no charge, but not its underlying Amazon EC2 instance metrics.

The option that says: The CloudWatch data metrics of the Auto Scaling group will be sent every five minutes with no additional charge is incorrect because basic monitoring only sends data metrics every 5 minutes, but Detailed Monitoring sends data metrics every minute.

questions.question:
A commercial bank plans to use AWS to host its application. The SysOps Administrator is instructed to create a CloudFormation template that will launch a large On-Demand EC2 Instance and install the 3rd-party application package. Once the instance has been launched and the application installed successfully, it should signal AWS CloudFormation that the process is complete. Conversely, it should also signal AWS CloudFormation if the installation fails.

Which of the following options should the Administrator use to achieve this requirement?
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which CloudFormation helper scripts install software on EC2 and signal success or failure back to CloudFormation?
choices: Use the cfn-init helper script to install the third-party package and send notification back to AWS CloudFormation.
Use the cfn-hup and cfn-init helper scripts.
Use the cfn-signal and cfn-init helper scripts.
Use the cfn-get-metadata and cfn-init helper scripts.
correctAnswers: Use the cfn-signal and cfn-init helper scripts.
questions.explanation:
The cfn-signal helper script signals AWS CloudFormation to indicate whether Amazon EC2 instances have been successfully created or updated. If you install and configure software applications on instances, you can signal AWS CloudFormation when those software applications are ready.

You use the cfn-signal script in conjunction with a CreationPolicy or an Auto Scaling group with a WaitOnResourceSignals update policy. When AWS CloudFormation creates or updates resources with those policies, it suspends work on the stack until the resource receives the requisite number of signals or until the timeout period is exceeded. For each valid signal that AWS CloudFormation receives, AWS CloudFormation publishes the signals to the stack events so that you track each signal.

Hence, the correct answer is: Use the cfn-signal and cfn-init helper scripts.

The option that says: Using the cfn-init helper script to install the third-party package and send notification back to AWS CloudFormation is incorrect because the cfn-init helper script is mainly used to read template metadata from the AWS::CloudFormation::Init key. Although this can be used to install software packages in the EC2 instance, you still need to use the cfn-signal helper script to indicate whether the Amazon EC2 instance and the 3rd party package have been successfully created.

The option that says: Using the cfn-get-metadata and cfn-init helper scripts is incorrect because the cfn-get-metadata helper script is mainly used to fetch a metadata block from AWS CloudFormation and print it to standard out.

The option that says: Using thecfn-hup and cfn-init helper scripts is incorrect because the cfn-hup helper script is basically a daemon that detects changes in resource metadata and runs user-specified actions when a change is detected.

questions.question:
A crowdfunding company has hired a CloudOps Engineer for consultation services. The company has set up many crowdfunding projects on its website using AWS Lambda, Amazon CloudFront, and Amazon S3. The company wants to add new features, such as logging statistical data on how often the website is accessed, measuring the success of crowdfunding projects, and implementing a way to check if employees are maliciously modifying website content.

Which of the following should be recommended to address these requests in a cost-effective way? (Select TWO.)
questions.simplifiedQuestion: Which two services cost-effectively provide access analytics and detect unauthorized content changes on a CloudFront-served site?
choices: Use CloudFront monitoring and usage reporting features to analyze access data and viewer data.
Associate a security group in the S3 bucket to secure and monitor all requests.
Turn on versioning and multi-factor authentication in S3 to see if contents are really being modified unwarily.
Use AWS CloudTrail to log all activity within the AWS account.
Create a Lambda function that periodically checks what projects are being accessed and how many users are accessing the website.
correctAnswers: Use CloudFront monitoring and usage reporting features to analyze access data and viewer data.
Use AWS CloudTrail to log all activity within the AWS account.
questions.explanation:
CloudTrail provides a record of actions taken by a user, role, or AWS service in CloudFront. Using the information collected by CloudTrail, you can determine the request that was made to CloudFront, the IP address from which the request was made, who made the request, when it was made, and additional details.

Monitoring is an important part of maintaining the availability and performance of CloudFront and your AWS solutions. You should collect monitoring data from all of the parts of your AWS solution so that you can more easily debug a multi-point failure if one occurs. AWS provides several tools for monitoring your CloudFront resources and activity and responding to potential incidents.

CloudFront monitoring

Hence, the correct answers are:

– Use CloudFront monitoring and usage reporting features to analyze access data and viewer data.

– Use AWS CloudTrail to log all activity within the AWS account.

The option that says: Associate a security group in the S3 bucket to secure and monitor all requests is incorrect because you can’t typically associate a security group in Amazon S3. This will not help you monitor and log user activities.

The option that says: Create a Lambda function that periodically checks what projects are being accessed and how many users are accessing the website is incorrect because doing this would be wasteful and costly, since you don’t need to periodically call the Lambda function to do such a service. AWS has other tools that serve the role better, such as AWS CloudTrail and CloudFront logging features.

The option that says: Turn on versioning and multi-factor authentication in S3 to see if contents are really being modified unwarily is incorrect because turning on versioning and MFA won’t help you achieve the requirements in the scenario. However, this is primarily good for securing the content.
